This prevents bugs due to incorrect semicolon insertions and ensures compatibility with tools with limited ASI support (e.g. clang-format).\n#### Const enums[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#enums)\nCode _must not_ use `const enum`; use plain `enum` instead.\nWhy?\nTypeScript enums already cannot be mutated; `const enum` is a separate language feature related to optimization that makes the enum invisible to JavaScript users of the module.\n#### Debugger statements[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#debugger-statements)\nDebugger statements _must not_ be included in production code.\n```\nfunction debugMe() {\n  debugger;\n}\n```\n\n####  `with`[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#diallowed-features-with)\nDo not use the `with` keyword. It makes your code harder to understand and [has been banned in strict mode since ES5](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Statements/with).\n#### Dynamic code evaluation[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#dynamic-code-evaluation)\nDo not use `eval` or the `Function(...string)` constructor (except for code loaders). These features are potentially dangerous and simply do not work in environments using strict [Content Security Policies](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/CSP).\n#### Non-standard features[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#non-standard-features)\nDo not use non-standard ECMAScript or Web Platform features.\nThis includes:\n  * Old features that have been marked deprecated or removed entirely from ECMAScript / the Web Platform (see [MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Deprecated_and_obsolete_features))\n  * New ECMAScript features that are not yet standardized \n    * Avoid using features that are in current TC39 working draft or currently in the [proposal process](https://tc39.es/process-document/)\n    * Use only ECMAScript features defined in the current ECMA-262 specification\n  * Proposed but not-yet-complete web standards: \n    * WHATWG proposals that have not completed the [proposal process](https://whatwg.org/faq#adding-new-features).\n  * Non-standard language \u201cextensions\u201d (such as those provided by some external transpilers)\n\n\nProjects targeting specific JavaScript runtimes, such as latest-Chrome-only, Chrome extensions, Node.JS, Electron, can obviously use those APIs. Use caution when considering an API surface that is proprietary and only implemented in some browsers; consider whether there is a common library that can abstract this API surface away for you.\n#### Modifying builtin objects[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#modifying-builtin-objects)\nNever modify builtin types, either by adding methods to their constructors or to their prototypes. Avoid depending on libraries that do this.\nDo not add symbols to the global object unless absolutely necessary (e.g. required by a third-party API).\n## Naming[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#naming)\n### Identifiers[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#identifiers)\nIdentifiers _must_ use only ASCII letters, digits, underscores (for constants and structured test method names), and (rarely) the '$' sign.\n#### Naming style[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#naming-style)\nTypeScript expresses information in types, so names _should not_ be decorated with information that is included in the type. (See also [Testing Blog](https://testing.googleblog.com/2017/10/code-health-identifiernamingpostforworl.html) for more about what not to include.)\nSome concrete examples of this rule:\n  * Do not use trailing or leading underscores for private properties or methods.\n  * Do not use the `opt_` prefix for optional parameters. \n    * For accessors, see [accessor rules](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#getters-and-setters-accessors) below.\n  * Do not mark interfaces specially (~~`IMyInterface`~~or~~`MyFooInterface`~~) unless it's idiomatic in its environment. When introducing an interface for a class, give it a name that expresses why the interface exists in the first place (e.g.`class TodoItem` and `interface TodoItemStorage` if the interface expresses the format used for storage/serialization in JSON).\n  * Suffixing `Observable`s with `$` is a common external convention and can help resolve confusion regarding observable values vs concrete values. Judgement on whether this is a useful convention is left up to individual teams, but _should_ be consistent within projects.\n\n\n#### Descriptive names[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#descriptive-names)\nNames _must_ be descriptive and clear to a new reader. Do not use abbreviations that are ambiguous or unfamiliar to readers outside your project, and do not abbreviate by deleting letters within a word.\n  * **Exception:** Variables that are in scope for 10 lines or fewer, including arguments that are _not_ part of an exported API, _may_ use short (e.g. single letter) variable names.\n\n```\n// Good identifiers:\nerrorCount          // No abbreviation.\ndnsConnectionIndex  // Most people know what \"DNS\" stands for.\nreferrerUrl         // Ditto for \"URL\".\ncustomerId          // \"Id\" is both ubiquitous and unlikely to be misunderstood.\n```\n```\n// Disallowed identifiers:\nn                   // Meaningless.\nnErr                // Ambiguous abbreviation.\nnCompConns          // Ambiguous abbreviation.\nwgcConnections      // Only your group knows what this stands for.\npcReader            // Lots of things can be abbreviated \"pc\".\ncstmrId             // Deletes internal letters.\nkSecondsPerDay      // Do not use Hungarian notation.\ncustomerID          // Incorrect camelcase of \"ID\".\n```\n\n#### Camel case[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#camel-case)\nTreat abbreviations like acronyms in names as whole words, i.e. use `loadHttpUrl`, not ~~`loadHTTPURL`~~, unless required by a platform name (e.g.`XMLHttpRequest`).\n#### Dollar sign[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#identifiers-dollar-sign)\nIdentifiers _should not_ generally use `$`, except when required by naming conventions for third party frameworks. [See above](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#naming-style) for more on using `$` with `Observable` values.\n### Rules by identifier type[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#naming-rules-by-identifier-type)\nMost identifier names should follow the casing in the table below, based on the identifier's type.\nStyle | Category  \n---|---  \n`UpperCamelCase` | class / interface / type / enum / decorator / type parameters / component functions in TSX / JSXElement type parameter  \n`lowerCamelCase` | variable / parameter / function / method / property / module alias  \n`CONSTANT_CASE` | global constant values, including enum values. See [Constants](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#identifiers-constants) below.  \n`#ident` | private identifiers are never used.  \n#### Type parameters[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#identifiers-type-parameters)\nType parameters, like in `Array<T>`, _may_ use a single upper case character (`T`) or `UpperCamelCase`.\n#### Test names[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#identifiers-test-names)\nTest method names inxUnit-style test frameworks _may_ be structured with `_` separators, e.g. `testX_whenY_doesZ()`.\n####  `_` prefix/suffix[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#identifiers-underscore-prefix-suffix)\nIdentifiers must not use `_` as a prefix or suffix.\nThis also means that `_` _must not_ be used as an identifier by itself (e.g. to indicate a parameter is unused).\n> Tip: If you only need some of the elements from an array (or TypeScript tuple), you can insert extra commas in a destructuring statement to ignore in-between elements:\n> ```\nconst [a, , b] = [1, 5, 10];  // a <- 1, b <- 10\n```\n\n#### Imports[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#identifiers-imports)\nModule namespace imports are `lowerCamelCase` while files are `snake_case`, which means that imports correctly will not match in casing style, such as\n```\nimport * as fooBar from './foo_bar';\n```\n\nSome libraries might commonly use a namespace import prefix that violates this naming scheme, but overbearingly common open source use makes the violating style more readable. The only libraries that currently fall under this exception are:\n  * [jquery](https://jquery.com/), using the `$` prefix\n  * [threejs](https://threejs.org/), using the `THREE` prefix\n\n\n#### Constants[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#identifiers-constants)\n**Immutable** : `CONSTANT_CASE` indicates that a value is _intended_ to not be changed, and _may_ be used for values that can technically be modified (i.e. values that are not deeply frozen) to indicate to users that they must not be modified.\n```\nconst UNIT_SUFFIXES = {\n  'milliseconds': 'ms',\n  'seconds': 's',\n};\n// Even though per the rules of JavaScript UNIT_SUFFIXES is\n// mutable, the uppercase shows users to not modify it.\n```\n\nA constant can also be a `static readonly` property of a class.\n```\nclass Foo {\n  private static readonly MY_SPECIAL_NUMBER = 5;\n\n  bar() {\n    return 2 * Foo.MY_SPECIAL_NUMBER;\n  }\n}\n```\n\n**Global** : Only symbols declared on the module level, static fields of module level classes, and values of module level enums, _may_ use `CONST_CASE`. If a value can be instantiated more than once over the lifetime of the program (e.g. a local variable declared within a function, or a static field on a class nested in a function) then it _must_ use `lowerCamelCase`.\nIf a value is an arrow function that implements an interface, then it _may_ be declared `lowerCamelCase`.\n#### Aliases[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#aliases)\nWhen creating a local-scope alias of an existing symbol, use the format of the existing identifier. The local alias _must_ match the existing naming and format of the source. For variables use `const` for your local aliases, and for class fields use the `readonly` attribute.\n> Note: If you're creating an alias just to expose it to a template in your framework of choice, remember to also apply the proper [access modifiers](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#properties-used-outside-of-class-lexical-scope).\n```\nconst {BrewStateEnum} = SomeType;\nconst CAPACITY = 5;\n\nclass Teapot {\n  readonly BrewStateEnum = BrewStateEnum;\n  readonly CAPACITY = CAPACITY;\n}\n```\n\n## Type system[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#type-system)\n### Type inference[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#type-inference)\nCode _may_ rely on type inference as implemented by the TypeScript compiler for all type expressions (variables, fields, return types, etc). \n```\nconst x = 15;  // Type inferred.\n```\n\nLeave out type annotations for trivially inferred types: variables or parameters initialized to a `string`, `number`, `boolean`, `RegExp` literal or `new` expression.\n```\nconst x: boolean = true;  // Bad: 'boolean' here does not aid readability\n```\n```\n// Bad: 'Set' is trivially inferred from the initialization\nconst x: Set<string> = new Set();\n```\n\nExplicitly specifying types may be required to prevent generic type parameters from being inferred as `unknown`. For example, initializing generic types with no values (e.g. empty arrays, objects, `Map`s, or `Set`s).\n```\nconst x = new Set<string>();\n```\n\nFor more complex expressions, type annotations can help with readability of the program:\n```\n// Hard to reason about the type of 'value' without an annotation.\nconst value = await rpc.getSomeValue().transform();\n```\n```\n// Can tell the type of 'value' at a glance.\nconst value: string[] = await rpc.getSomeValue().transform();\n```\n\nWhether an annotation is required is decided by the code reviewer.\n#### Return types[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#return-types)\nWhether to include return type annotations for functions and methods is up to the code author. Reviewers _may_ ask for annotations to clarify complex return types that are hard to understand. Projects _may_ have a local policy to always require return types, but this is not a general TypeScript style requirement.\nThere are two benefits to explicitly typing out the implicit return values of functions and methods:\n  * More precise documentation to benefit readers of the code.\n  * Surface potential type errors faster in the future if there are code changes that change the return type of the function.\n\n\n### Undefined and null[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#undefined-and-null)\nTypeScript supports `undefined` and `null` types. Nullable types can be constructed as a union type (`string|null`); similarly with `undefined`. There is no special syntax for unions of `undefined` and `null`.\nTypeScript code can use either `undefined` or `null` to denote absence of a value, there is no general guidance to prefer one over the other. Many JavaScript APIs use `undefined` (e.g. `Map.get`), while many DOM and Google APIs use `null` (e.g. `Element.getAttribute`), so the appropriate absent value depends on the context.\n#### Nullable/undefined type aliases[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#nullableundefined-type-aliases)\nType aliases _must not_ include `|null` or `|undefined` in a union type. Nullable aliases typically indicate that null values are being passed around through too many layers of an application, and this clouds the source of the original issue that resulted in `null`. They also make it unclear when specific values on a class or interface might be absent.\nInstead, code _must_ only add `|null` or `|undefined` when the alias is actually used. Code _should_ deal with null values close to where they arise, using the above techniques.\n```\n// Bad\ntype CoffeeResponse = Latte|Americano|undefined;\n\nclass CoffeeService {\n  getLatte(): CoffeeResponse { ... };\n}\n```\n```\n// Better\ntype CoffeeResponse = Latte|Americano;\n\nclass CoffeeService {\n  getLatte(): CoffeeResponse|undefined { ... };\n}\n```\n\n#### Prefer optional over `|undefined`[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#prefer-optional-over-undefined)\nIn addition, TypeScript supports a special construct for optional parameters and fields, using `?`:\n```\ninterface CoffeeOrder {\n  sugarCubes: number;\n  milk?: Whole|LowFat|HalfHalf;\n}\n\nfunction pourCoffee(volume?: Milliliter) { ... }\n```\n\nOptional parameters implicitly include `|undefined` in their type. However, they are different in that they can be left out when constructing a value or calling a method. For example, `{sugarCubes: 1}` is a valid `CoffeeOrder` because `milk` is optional.\nUse optional fields (on interfaces or classes) and parameters rather than a `|undefined` type.\nFor classes preferably avoid this pattern altogether and initialize as many fields as possible.\n```\nclass MyClass {\n  field = '';\n}\n```\n\n### Use structural types[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#use-structural-types)\nTypeScript's type system is structural, not nominal. That is, a value matches a type if it has at least all the properties the type requires and the properties' types match, recursively.\nWhen providing a structural-based implementation, explicitly include the type at the declaration of the symbol (this allows more precise type checking and error reporting).\n```\nconst foo: Foo = {\n  a: 123,\n  b: 'abc',\n}\n```\n```\nconst badFoo = {\n  a: 123,\n  b: 'abc',\n}\n```\n\nUse interfaces to define structural types, not classes\n```\ninterface Foo {\n  a: number;\n  b: string;\n}\n\nconst foo: Foo = {\n  a: 123,\n  b: 'abc',\n}\n```\n```\nclass Foo {\n  readonly a: number;\n  readonly b: number;\n}\n\nconst foo: Foo = {\n  a: 123,\n  b: 'abc',\n}\n```\n\nWhy?\nThe \"badFoo\" object above relies on type inference. Additional fields could be added to \"badFoo\" and the type is inferred based on the object itself.\nWhen passing a \"badFoo\" to a function that takes a \"Foo\", the error will be at the function call site, rather than at the object declaration site. This is also useful when changing the surface of an interface across broad codebases.\n```\ninterface Animal {\n  sound: string;\n  name: string;\n}\n\nfunction makeSound(animal: Animal) {}\n\n/**\n * 'cat' has an inferred type of '{sound: string}'\n */\nconst cat = {\n  sound: 'meow',\n};\n\n/**\n * 'cat' does not meet the type contract required for the function, so the\n * TypeScript compiler errors here, which may be very far from where 'cat' is\n * defined.\n */\nmakeSound(cat);\n\n/**\n * Horse has a structural type and the type error shows here rather than the\n * function call.  'horse' does not meet the type contract of 'Animal'.\n */\nconst horse: Animal = {\n  sound: 'niegh',\n};\n\nconst dog: Animal = {\n  sound: 'bark',\n  name: 'MrPickles',\n};\n\nmakeSound(dog);\nmakeSound(horse);\n```\n\n### Prefer interfaces over type literal aliases[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#prefer-interfaces)\nTypeScript supports [type aliases](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/everyday-types.html#type-aliases) for naming a type expression. This can be used to name primitives, unions, tuples, and any other types.\nHowever, when declaring types for objects, use interfaces instead of a type alias for the object literal expression.\n```\ninterface User {\n  firstName: string;\n  lastName: string;\n}\n```\n```\ntype User = {\n  firstName: string,\n  lastName: string,\n}\n```\n\nWhy?\nThese forms are nearly equivalent, so under the principle of just choosing one out of two forms to prevent variation, we should choose one. Additionally, there are also [interesting technical reasons to prefer interface](https://ncjamieson.com/prefer-interfaces/). That page quotes the TypeScript team lead: \"Honestly, my take is that it should really just be interfaces for anything that they can model. There is no benefit to type aliases when there are so many issues around display/perf.\"\n###  `Array<T>` Type[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#arrayt-type)\nFor simple types (containing just alphanumeric characters and dot), use the syntax sugar for arrays, `T[]` or `readonly T[]`, rather than the longer form `Array<T>` or `ReadonlyArray<T>`.\nFor multi-dimensional non-`readonly` arrays of simple types, use the syntax sugar form (`T[][]`, `T[][][]`, and so on) rather than the longer form.\nFor anything more complex, use the longer form `Array<T>`.\nThese rules apply at each level of nesting, i.e. a simple `T[]` nested in a more complex type would still be spelled as `T[]`, using the syntax sugar.\n```\nlet a: string[];\nlet b: readonly string[];\nlet c: ns.MyObj[];\nlet d: string[][];\nlet e: Array<{n: number, s: string}>;\nlet f: Array<string|number>;\nlet g: ReadonlyArray<string|number>;\nlet h: InjectionToken<string[]>;  // Use syntax sugar for nested types.\nlet i: ReadonlyArray<string[]>;\nlet j: Array<readonly string[]>;\n```\n```\nlet a: Array<string>;  // The syntax sugar is shorter.\nlet b: ReadonlyArray<string>;\nlet c: Array<ns.MyObj>;\nlet d: Array<string[]>;\nlet e: {n: number, s: string}[];  // The braces make it harder to read.\nlet f: (string|number)[];         // Likewise with parens.\nlet g: readonly (string | number)[];\nlet h: InjectionToken<Array<string>>;\nlet i: readonly string[][];\nlet j: (readonly string[])[];\n```\n\n### Indexable types / index signatures (`{[key: string]: T}`)[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#indexable-key-string-type)\nIn JavaScript, it's common to use an object as an associative array (aka \"map\", \"hash\", or \"dict\"). Such objects can be typed using an [index signature](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/objects.html#index-signatures) (`[k: string]: T`) in TypeScript:\n```\nconst fileSizes: {[fileName: string]: number} = {};\nfileSizes['readme.txt'] = 541;\n```\n\nIn TypeScript, provide a meaningful label for the key. (The label only exists for documentation; it's unused otherwise.)\n```\nconst users: {[key: string]: number} = ...;\n```\n```\nconst users: {[userName: string]: number} = ...;\n```\n\n> Rather than using one of these, consider using the ES6 `Map` and `Set` types instead. JavaScript objects have [surprising undesirable behaviors](http://2ality.com/2012/01/objects-as-maps.html) and the ES6 types more explicitly convey your intent. Also, `Map`s can be keyed by\u2014and `Set`s can contain\u2014types other than `string`.\nTypeScript's builtin `Record<Keys, ValueType>` type allows constructing types with a defined set of keys. This is distinct from associative arrays in that the keys are statically known. See advice on that [below](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#mapped-conditional-types).\n### Mapped and conditional types[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#mapped-conditional-types)\nTypeScript's [mapped types](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/mapped-types.html) and [conditional types](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/conditional-types.html) allow specifying new types based on other types. TypeScript's standard library includes several type operators based on these (`Record`, `Partial`, `Readonly` etc).\nThese type system features allow succinctly specifying types and constructing powerful yet type safe abstractions. They come with a number of drawbacks though:\n  * Compared to explicitly specifying properties and type relations (e.g. using interfaces and extension, see below for an example), type operators require the reader to mentally evaluate the type expression. This can make programs substantially harder to read, in particular combined with type inference and expressions crossing file boundaries.\n  * Mapped & conditional types' evaluation model, in particular when combined with type inference, is underspecified, not always well understood, and often subject to change in TypeScript compiler versions. Code can \"accidentally\" compile or seem to give the right results. This increases future support cost of code using type operators.\n  * Mapped & conditional types are most powerful when deriving types from complex and/or inferred types. On the flip side, this is also when they are most prone to create hard to understand and maintain programs.\n  * Some language tooling does not work well with these type system features. E.g. your IDE's find references (and thus rename property refactoring) will not find properties in a `Pick<T, Keys>` type, and Code Search won't hyperlink them.\n\n\nThe style recommendation is:\n  * Always use the simplest type construct that can possibly express your code.\n  * A little bit of repetition or verbosity is often much cheaper than the long term cost of complex type expressions.\n  * Mapped & conditional types may be used, subject to these considerations.\n\n\nFor example, TypeScript's builtin `Pick<T, Keys>` type allows creating a new type by subsetting another type `T`, but simple interface extension can often be easier to understand.\n```\ninterface User {\n  shoeSize: number;\n  favoriteIcecream: string;\n  favoriteChocolate: string;\n}\n\n// FoodPreferences has favoriteIcecream and favoriteChocolate, but not shoeSize.\ntype FoodPreferences = Pick<User, 'favoriteIcecream'|'favoriteChocolate'>;\n```\n\nThis is equivalent to spelling out the properties on `FoodPreferences`:\n```\ninterface FoodPreferences {\n  favoriteIcecream: string;\n  favoriteChocolate: string;\n}\n```\n\nTo reduce duplication, `User` could extend `FoodPreferences`, or (possibly better) nest a field for food preferences:\n```\ninterface FoodPreferences { /* as above */ }\ninterface User extends FoodPreferences {\n  shoeSize: number;\n  // also includes the preferences.\n}\n```\n\nUsing interfaces here makes the grouping of properties explicit, improves IDE support, allows better optimization, and arguably makes the code easier to understand.\n###  `any` Type[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#any)\nTypeScript's `any` type is a super and subtype of all other types, and allows dereferencing all properties. As such, `any` is dangerous - it can mask severe programming errors, and its use undermines the value of having static types in the first place.\n**Consider _not_ to use `any`.** In circumstances where you want to use `any`, consider one of:\n  * [Provide a more specific type](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#any-specific)\n  * [Use `unknown`](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#any-unknown)\n  * [Suppress the lint warning and document why](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#any-suppress)\n\n\n#### Providing a more specific type[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#any-specific)\nUse interfaces , an inline object type, or a type alias:\n```\n// Use declared interfaces to represent server-side JSON.\ndeclare interface MyUserJson {\n  name: string;\n  email: string;\n}\n\n// Use type aliases for types that are repetitive to write.\ntype MyType = number|string;\n\n// Or use inline object types for complex returns.\nfunction getTwoThings(): {something: number, other: string} {\n  // ...\n  return {something, other};\n}\n\n// Use a generic type, where otherwise a library would say `any` to represent\n// they don't care what type the user is operating on (but note \"Return type\n// only generics\" below).\nfunction nicestElement<T>(items: T[]): T {\n  // Find the nicest element in items.\n  // Code can also put constraints on T, e.g. <T extends HTMLElement>.\n}\n```\n\n#### Using `unknown` over `any`[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#any-unknown)\nThe `any` type allows assignment into any other type and dereferencing any property off it. Often this behaviour is not necessary or desirable, and code just needs to express that a type is unknown. Use the built-in type `unknown` in that situation \u2014 it expresses the concept and is much safer as it does not allow dereferencing arbitrary properties.\n```\n// Can assign any value (including null or undefined) into this but cannot\n// use it without narrowing the type or casting.\nconst val: unknown = value;\n```\n```\nconst danger: any = value /* result of an arbitrary expression */;\ndanger.whoops();  // This access is completely unchecked!\n```\n\nTo safely use `unknown` values, narrow the type using a [type guard](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/advanced-types.html#type-guards-and-differentiating-types)\n#### Suppressing `any` lint warnings[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#any-suppress)\nSometimes using `any` is legitimate, for example in tests to construct a mock object. In such cases, add a comment that suppresses the lint warning, and document why it is legitimate.\n```\n// This test only needs a partial implementation of BookService, and if\n// we overlooked something the test will fail in an obvious way.\n// This is an intentionally unsafe partial mock\n// tslint:disable-next-line:no-any\nconst mockBookService = ({get() { return mockBook; }} as any) as BookService;\n// Shopping cart is not used in this test\n// tslint:disable-next-line:no-any\nconst component = new MyComponent(mockBookService, /* unused ShoppingCart */ null as any);\n```\n\n###  `{}` Type[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#empty-interface-type)\nThe `{}` type, also known as an _empty interface_ type, represents a interface with no properties. An empty interface type has no specified properties and therefore any non-nullish value is assignable to it.\n```\nlet player: {};\n\nplayer = {\n  health: 50,\n}; // Allowed.\n\nconsole.log(player.health) // Property 'health' does not exist on type '{}'.\n```\n```\nfunction takeAnything(obj:{}) {\n\n}\n\ntakeAnything({});\ntakeAnything({ a: 1, b: 2 });\n```\n\nGoogle3 code **should not** use `{}` for most use cases. `{}` represents any non-nullish primitive or object type, which is rarely appropriate. Prefer one of the following more-descriptive types:\n  * `unknown` can hold any value, including `null` or `undefined`, and is generally more appropriate for opaque values.\n  * `Record<string, T>` is better for dictionary-like objects, and provides better type safety by being explicit about the type `T` of contained values (which may itself be `unknown`).\n  * `object` excludes primitives as well, leaving only non-nullish functions and objects, but without any other assumptions about what properties may be available.\n\n\n### Tuple types[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#tuple-types)\nIf you are tempted to create a Pair type, instead use a tuple type:\n```\ninterface Pair {\n  first: string;\n  second: string;\n}\nfunction splitInHalf(input: string): Pair {\n  ...\n  return {first: x, second: y};\n}\n```\n```\nfunction splitInHalf(input: string): [string, string] {\n  ...\n  return [x, y];\n}\n\n// Use it like:\nconst [leftHalf, rightHalf] = splitInHalf('my string');\n```\n\nHowever, often it's clearer to provide meaningful names for the properties.\nIf declaring an `interface` is too heavyweight, you can use an inline object literal type:\n```\nfunction splitHostPort(address: string): {host: string, port: number} {\n  ...\n}\n\n// Use it like:\nconst address = splitHostPort(userAddress);\nuse(address.port);\n\n// You can also get tuple-like behavior using destructuring:\nconst {host, port} = splitHostPort(userAddress);\n```\n\n### Wrapper types[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#wrapper-types)\nThere are a few types related to JavaScript primitives that _should not_ ever be used:\n  * `String`, `Boolean`, and `Number` have slightly different meaning from the corresponding primitive types `string`, `boolean`, and `number`. Always use the lowercase version.\n  * `Object` has similarities to both `{}` and `object`, but is slightly looser. Use `{}` for a type that include everything except `null` and `undefined`, or lowercase `object` to further exclude the other primitive types (the three mentioned above, plus `symbol` and `bigint`).\n\n\nFurther, never invoke the wrapper types as constructors (with `new`).\n### Return type only generics[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#return-type-only-generics)\nAvoid creating APIs that have return type only generics. When working with existing APIs that have return type only generics always explicitly specify the generics.\n## Toolchain requirements[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#toolchain-requirements)\nGoogle style requires using a number of tools in specific ways, outlined here.\n### TypeScript compiler[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#typescript-compiler)\nAll TypeScript files must pass type checking using the standard tool chain.\n#### @ts-ignore[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#ts-ignore)\nDo not use `@ts-ignore` nor the variants `@ts-expect-error` or `@ts-nocheck`.\nWhy?\nThey superficially seem to be an easy way to \"fix\" a compiler error, but in practice, a specific compiler error is often caused by a larger problem that can be fixed more directly.\nFor example, if you are using `@ts-ignore` to suppress a type error, then it's hard to predict what types the surrounding code will end up seeing. For many type errors, the advice in [how to best use `any`](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#any) is useful.\nYou may use `@ts-expect-error` in unit tests, though you generally _should not_. `@ts-expect-error` suppresses all errors. It's easy to accidentally over-match and suppress more serious errors. Consider one of:\n  * When testing APIs that need to deal with unchecked values at runtime, add casts to the expected type or to `any` and add an explanatory comment. This limits error suppression to a single expression.\n  * Suppress the lint warning and document why, similar to [suppressing `any` lint warnings](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#any-suppress).\n\n\n### Conformance[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#conformance)\nGoogle TypeScript includes several _conformance frameworks_ , [tsetse](https://tsetse.info) and [tsec](https://github.com/google/tsec).\nThese rules are commonly used to enforce critical restrictions (such as defining globals, which could break the codebase) and security patterns (such as using `eval` or assigning to `innerHTML`), or more loosely to improve code quality.\nGoogle-style TypeScript must abide by any applicable global or framework-local conformance rules.\n## Comments and documentation[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#comments-documentation)\n#### JSDoc versus comments[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#jsdoc-vs-comments)\nThere are two types of comments, JSDoc (`/** ... */`) and non-JSDoc ordinary comments (`// ...` or `/* ... */`).\n  * Use `/** JSDoc */` comments for documentation, i.e. comments a user of the code should read.\n  * Use `// line comments` for implementation comments, i.e. comments that only concern the implementation of the code itself.\n\n\nJSDoc comments are understood by tools (such as editors and documentation generators), while ordinary comments are only for other humans.\n#### Multi-line comments[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#multi-line-comments)\nMulti-line comments are indented at the same level as the surrounding code. They _must_ use multiple single-line comments (`//`-style), not block comment style (`/* */`).\n```\n// This is\n// fine\n```\n```\n/*\n * This should\n * use multiple\n * single-line comments\n */\n\n/* This should use // */\n```\n\nComments are not enclosed in boxes drawn with asterisks or other characters.\n### JSDoc general form[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#jsdoc-general-form)\nThe basic formatting of JSDoc comments is as seen in this example:\n```\n/**\n * Multiple lines of JSDoc text are written here,\n * wrapped normally.\n * @param arg A number to do something to.\n */\nfunction doSomething(arg: number) { \u2026 }\n```\n\nor in this single-line example:\n```\n/** This short jsdoc describes the function. */\nfunction doSomething(arg: number) { \u2026 }\n```\n\nIf a single-line comment overflows into multiple lines, it _must_ use the multi-line style with `/**` and `*/` on their own lines.\nMany tools extract metadata from JSDoc comments to perform code validation and optimization. As such, these comments _must_ be well-formed.\n### Markdown[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#jsdoc-markdown)\nJSDoc is written in Markdown, though it _may_ include HTML when necessary.\nThis means that tooling parsing JSDoc will ignore plain text formatting, so if you did this:\n```\n/**\n * Computes weight based on three factors:\n *   items sent\n *   items received\n *   last timestamp\n */\n```\n\nit will be rendered like this:\n```\nComputes weight based on three factors: items sent items received last timestamp\n\n```\n\nInstead, write a Markdown list:\n```\n/**\n * Computes weight based on three factors:\n *\n * - items sent\n * - items received\n * - last timestamp\n */\n```\n\n### JSDoc tags[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#jsdoc-tags)\nGoogle style allows a subset of JSDoc tags. Most tags must occupy their own line, with the tag at the beginning of the line.\n```\n/**\n * The \"param\" tag must occupy its own line and may not be combined.\n * @param left A description of the left param.\n * @param right A description of the right param.\n */\nfunction add(left: number, right: number) { ... }\n```\n```\n/**\n * The \"param\" tag must occupy its own line and may not be combined.\n * @param left @param right\n */\nfunction add(left: number, right: number) { ... }\n```\n\n### Line wrapping[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#jsdoc-line-wrapping)\nLine-wrapped block tags are indented four spaces. Wrapped description text _may_ be lined up with the description on previous lines, but this horizontal alignment is discouraged.\n```\n/**\n * Illustrates line wrapping for long param/return descriptions.\n * @param foo This is a param with a particularly long description that just\n *     doesn't fit on one line.\n * @return This returns something that has a lengthy description too long to fit\n *     in one line.\n */\nexports.method = function(foo) {\n  return 5;\n};\n```\n\nDo not indent when wrapping a `@desc` or `@fileoverview` description.\n### Document all top-level exports of modules[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#document-all-top-level-exports-of-modules)\nUse `/** JSDoc */` comments to communicate information to the users of your code. Avoid merely restating the property or parameter name. You _should_ also document all properties and methods (exported/public or not) whose purpose is not immediately obvious from their name, as judged by your reviewer.\n**Exception:** Symbols that are only exported to be consumed by tooling, such as @NgModule classes, do not require comments.\n### Class comments[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#jsdoc-class-comments)\nJSDoc comments for classes should provide the reader with enough information to know how and when to use the class, as well as any additional considerations necessary to correctly use the class. Textual descriptions may be omitted on the constructor.\n### Method and function comments[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#method-and-function-comments)\nMethod, parameter, and return descriptions may be omitted if they are obvious from the rest of the method\u2019s JSDoc or from the method name and type signature.\nMethod descriptions begin with a verb phrase that describes what the method does. This phrase is not an imperative sentence, but instead is written in the third person, as if there is an implied \"This method ...\" before it.\n### Parameter property comments[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#parameter-property-comments)\nA [parameter property](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/classes.html#parameter-properties) is a constructor parameter that is prefixed by one of the modifiers `private`, `protected`, `public`, or `readonly`. A parameter property declares both a parameter and an instance property, and implicitly assigns into it. For example, `constructor(private readonly foo: Foo)`, declares that the constructor takes a parameter `foo`, but also declares a private readonly property `foo`, and assigns the parameter into that property before executing the remainder of the constructor.\nTo document these fields, use JSDoc's `@param` annotation. Editors display the description on constructor calls and property accesses.\n```\n/** This class demonstrates how parameter properties are documented. */\nclass ParamProps {\n  /**\n   * @param percolator The percolator used for brewing.\n   * @param beans The beans to brew.\n   */\n  constructor(\n    private readonly percolator: Percolator,\n    private readonly beans: CoffeeBean[]) {}\n}\n```\n```\n/** This class demonstrates how ordinary fields are documented. */\nclass OrdinaryClass {\n  /** The bean that will be used in the next call to brew(). */\n  nextBean: CoffeeBean;\n\n  constructor(initialBean: CoffeeBean) {\n    this.nextBean = initialBean;\n  }\n}\n```\n\n### JSDoc type annotations[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#jsdoc-type-annotations)\nJSDoc type annotations are redundant in TypeScript source code. Do not declare types in `@param` or `@return` blocks, do not write `@implements`, `@enum`, `@private`, `@override` etc. on code that uses the `implements`, `enum`, `private`, `override` etc. keywords.\n### Make comments that actually add information[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#redundant-comments)\nFor non-exported symbols, sometimes the name and type of the function or parameter is enough. Code will _usually_ benefit from more documentation than just variable names though!\n  * Avoid comments that just restate the parameter name and type, e.g.\n```\n/** @param fooBarService The Bar service for the Foo application. */\n```\n\n  * Because of this rule, `@param` and `@return` lines are only required when they add information, and _may_ otherwise be omitted.\n```\n/**\n * POSTs the request to start coffee brewing.\n * @param amountLitres The amount to brew. Must fit the pot size!\n */\nbrew(amountLitres: number, logger: Logger) {\n  // ...\n}\n```\n\n\n\n#### Comments when calling a function[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#comments-when-calling-a-function)\n\u201cParameter name\u201d comments should be used whenever the method name and parameter value do not sufficiently convey the meaning of the parameter.\nBefore adding these comments, consider refactoring the method to instead accept an interface and destructure it to greatly improve call-site readability.\n\"Parameter name\" comments go before the parameter value, and include the parameter name and a `=` suffix:\n```\nsomeFunction(obviousParam, /* shouldRender= */ true, /* name= */ 'hello');\n```\n\nExisting code may use a legacy parameter name comment style, which places these comments ~after~ the parameter value and omits the `=`. Continuing to use this style within the file for consistency is acceptable.\n```\nsomeFunction(obviousParam, true /* shouldRender */, 'hello' /* name */);\n```\n\n### Place documentation prior to decorators[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#place-documentation-prior-to-decorators)\nWhen a class, method, or property have both decorators like `@Component` and JsDoc, please make sure to write the JsDoc before the decorator.\n  * Do not write JsDoc between the Decorator and the decorated statement.\n```\n@Component({\n  selector: 'foo',\n  template: 'bar',\n})\n/** Component that prints \"bar\". */\nexport class FooComponent {}\n```\n\n  * Write the JsDoc block before the Decorator.\n```\n/** Component that prints \"bar\". */\n@Component({\n  selector: 'foo',\n  template: 'bar',\n})\nexport class FooComponent {}\n```\n\n\n\n## Policies[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#policies)\n### Consistency[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#consistency)\nFor any style question that isn't settled definitively by this specification, do what the other code in the same file is already doing (\"be consistent\"). If that doesn't resolve the question, consider emulating the other files in the same directory.\nBrand new files _must_ use Google Style, regardless of the style choices of other files in the same package. When adding new code to a file that is not in Google Style, reformatting the existing code first is recommended, subject to the advice [below](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#reformatting-existing-code). If this reformatting is not done, then new code _should_ be as consistent as possible with existing code in the same file, but _must not_ violate the style guide.\n#### Reformatting existing code[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#reformatting-existing-code)\nYou will occasionally encounter files in the codebase that are not in proper Google Style. These may have come from an acquisition, or may have been written before Google Style took a position on some issue, or may be in non-Google Style for any other reason.\nWhen updating the style of existing code, follow these guidelines.\n  1. It is not required to change all existing code to meet current style guidelines. Reformatting existing code is a trade-off between code churn and consistency. Style rules evolve over time and these kinds of tweaks to maintain compliance would create unnecessary churn. However, if significant changes are being made to a file it is expected that the file will be in Google Style.\n  2. Be careful not to allow opportunistic style fixes to muddle the focus of a CL. If you find yourself making a lot of style changes that aren\u2019t critical to the central focus of a CL, promote those changes to a separate CL.\n\n\n### Deprecation[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#deprecation)\nMark deprecated methods, classes or interfaces with an `@deprecated` JSDoc annotation. A deprecation comment must include simple, clear directions for people to fix their call sites.\n### Generated code: mostly exempt[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#generated-code)\nSource code generated by the build process is not required to be in Google Style. However, any generated identifiers that will be referenced from hand-written source code must follow the naming requirements. As a special exception, such identifiers are allowed to contain underscores, which may help to avoid conflicts with hand-written identifiers.\n#### Style guide goals[![](https://google.github.io/styleguide/include/link.png)](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#style-guide-goals)\nIn general, engineers usually know best about what's needed in their code, so if there are multiple options and the choice is situation dependent, we should let decisions be made locally. So the default answer should be \"leave it out\".\nThe following points are the exceptions, which are the reasons we have some global rules. Evaluate your style guide proposal against the following:\n  1. **Code should avoid patterns that are known to cause problems, especially for users new to the language.**\n  2. **Code across projects should be consistent across irrelevant variations.**\nWhen there are two options that are equivalent in a superficial way, we should consider choosing one just so we don't divergently evolve for no reason and avoid pointless debates in code reviews.\nExamples:\n     * The capitalization style of names.\n     * `x as T` syntax vs the equivalent `<T>x` syntax (disallowed).\n     * `Array<[number, number]>` vs `[number, number][]`.\n  3. **Code should be maintainable in the long term.**\nCode usually lives longer than the original author works on it, and the TypeScript team must keep all of Google working into the future.\nExamples:\n     * We use software to automate changes to code, so code is autoformatted so it's easy for software to meet whitespace rules.\n     * We require a single set of compiler flags, so a given TS library can be written assuming a specific set of flags, and users can always safely use a shared library.\n     * Code must import the libraries it uses (\"strict deps\") so that a refactor in a dependency doesn't change the dependencies of its users.\n     * We ask users to write tests. Without tests we cannot have confidence that changes that we make to the language, don't break users.\n  4. **Code reviewers should be focused on improving the quality of the code, not enforcing arbitrary rules.**\nIf it's possible to implement your rule as an automated check that is often a good sign. This also supports principle 3.\nIf it really just doesn't matter that much -- if it's an obscure corner of the language or if it avoids a bug that is unlikely to occur -- it's probably worth leaving out.\n\n\n* * *\n  1. Namespace imports are often called 'module imports' [\u21a9](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#fnref1)\n  2. named imports are sometimes called 'destructuring imports' because they use similar syntax to destructuring assignments. [\u21a9](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html#fnref2)\n\n\n", "TS Dev Style Guide": "[Services](https://ts.dev/#services) [Technologies](https://ts.dev/#technologies) [Contacts](https://ts.dev/#contacts) [TS Style Guide](https://ts.dev/style/)\n[![TS.dev logo](https://ts.dev/svg/logo.svg) ](https://ts.dev/)[Get in touch ![contact](https://ts.dev/svg/mail.svg)](https://ts.dev/#contacts)Navigation\n  1. [Function Expressions](https://ts.dev/style/#function-expressions)\n      1. [Use arrow functions in expressions](https://ts.dev/style/#use-arrow-functions-in-expressions)\n      2. [Expression bodies vs block bodies](https://ts.dev/style/#expression-bodies-vs-block-bodies)\n      3. [Rebinding this](https://ts.dev/style/#rebinding-this)\n      4. [Namespaces vs Modules](https://ts.dev/style/#namespaces-vs-modules)\n    2. [Exports](https://ts.dev/style/#exports)\n      1. [Export visibility](https://ts.dev/style/#export-visibility)\n      2. [Mutable Exports](https://ts.dev/style/#mutable-exports)\n      3. [Container Classes](https://ts.dev/style/#container-classes)\n    3. [Imports](https://ts.dev/style/#imports)\n      1. [Module versus destructuring imports](https://ts.dev/style/#module-versus-destructuring-imports)\n      2. [Renaming imports](https://ts.dev/style/#renaming-imports)\n      3. [Import & export type](https://ts.dev/style/#import-export-type)\n    4. [Organize By Feature](https://ts.dev/style/#organize-by-feature)\n  4. [Type System](https://ts.dev/style/#type-system)\n    1. [Type Inference](https://ts.dev/style/#type-inference)\n      1. [Return types](https://ts.dev/style/#return-types)\n    2. [Null vs Undefined](https://ts.dev/style/#null-vs-undefined)\n      1. [Nullable/undefined type aliases](https://ts.dev/style/#nullableundefined-type-aliases)\n      2. [Optionals vs |undefined type](https://ts.dev/style/#optionals-vs-undefined-type)\n    3. [Structural Types vs Nominal Types](https://ts.dev/style/#structural-types-vs-nominal-types)\n    4. [Interfaces vs Type Aliases](https://ts.dev/style/#interfaces-vs-type-aliases)\n    5. [ArrayType](https://ts.dev/style/#arrayt-type)\n    6. [Indexable ({[key: string]: number}) Type](https://ts.dev/style/#indexable-key-string-number-type)\n    7. [Mapped & Conditional Types](https://ts.dev/style/#mapped-conditional-types)\n    8. [any Type](https://ts.dev/style/#any)\n      1. [Providing a more specific type](https://ts.dev/style/#any-specific)\n      2. [Using unknown over any](https://ts.dev/style/#any-unknown)\n      3. [Suppressing any lint warnings](https://ts.dev/style/#any-suppress)\n    9. [Tuple Types](https://ts.dev/style/#tuple-types)\n    10. [Wrapper types](https://ts.dev/style/#wrapper-types)\n    11. [Return type only generics](https://ts.dev/style/#return-type-only-generics)\n  5. [Consistency](https://ts.dev/style/#consistency)\n  6. [Changing the style guide](https://ts.dev/style/#changes)\n    1. [Goals](https://ts.dev/style/#goals)\n    2. [Submitting Changes](https://ts.dev/style/#changes-discuss)\n\n\n# TypeScript style guide\nThis is the style guide for the TypeScript language that was based on the one that is provided by Google. It contains both rules and best practices. Choose those that work best for your team.\nIf you have a suggestion on how to improve this style guide please check the [Changing the style guide](https://ts.dev/style/#changes) below.\nThis Style Guide uses [RFC 2119](http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2119) terminology when using the phrases _must_ , _must not_ , _should_ , _should not_ , and _may_. All examples given are non-normative and serve only to illustrate the normative language of the style guide.\n## Syntax[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#syntax)\n### Identifiers[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#identifiers)\nIdentifiers must use only ASCII letters, digits, underscores (for constants and structured test method names), and the '$' sign. Thus each valid identifier name is matched by the regular expression `[$\\w]+`.\nStyle | Category  \n---|---  \n`UpperCamelCase` | class / interface / type / enum / decorator / type / parameters  \n`lowerCamelCase` | variable / parameter / function / method / property / module alias  \n`CONSTANT_CASE` | global constant values, including enum values  \n`#ident` | private identifiers are never used.  \n  * **Abbreviations** : Treat abbreviations like acronyms in names as whole words, i.e. use `loadHttpUrl`, not ~~`loadHTTPURL`~~, unless required by a platform name (e.g.`XMLHttpRequest`).\n  * **Dollar sign** : Identifiers _should not_ generally use `$`, except when aligning with naming conventions for third party frameworks. [See below](https://ts.dev/style/#naming-style) for more on using `$` with `Observable` values.\n  * **Type parameters** : Type parameters, like in `Array<T>`, may use a single upper case character (`T`) or `UpperCamelCase`.\n  * **Test names** : Test method names in Closure `testSuite`s and similar xUnit-style test frameworks may be structured with `_` separators, e.g. `testX_whenY_doesZ()`.\n  * **`_`prefix/suffix** : Identifiers must not use `_` as a prefix or suffix.\nThis also means that `_` must not be used as an identifier by itself (e.g. to indicate a parameter is unused).\n\n\n> Tip: If you only need some of the elements from an array (or TypeScript tuple), you can insert extra commas in a destructuring statement to ignore in-between elements:\n> ```\n\n> \nconst [a, , b] = [1, 5, 10]; // a <- 1, b <- 10\n\n> \n```\n\n  * **Imports** : Module namespace imports are `lowerCamelCase` while files are `snake_case`, which means that imports correctly will not match in casing style, such as\n```\n\n\nimport * as fooBar from \"./foo_bar\";\n\n\n```\n\nSome libraries might commonly use a namespace import prefix that violates this naming scheme, but overbearingly common open source use makes the violating style more readable. The only libraries that currently fall under this exception are:\n    * [jquery](https://jquery.com/), using the `$` prefix\n    * [threejs](https://threejs.org/), using the `THREE` prefix\n  * **Constants** : `CONSTANT_CASE` indicates that a value is _intended_ to not be changed, and may be used for values that can technically be modified (i.e. values that are not deeply frozen) to indicate to users that they must not be modified.\n```\n\n\nconst UNIT_SUFFIXES = {\n  milliseconds: \"ms\",\n  seconds: \"s\",\n};\n// Even though per the rules of JavaScript UNIT_SUFFIXES is\n// mutable, the uppercase shows users to not modify it.\n\n\n```\n\nA constant can also be a `static readonly` property of a class.\n```\n\n\nclass Foo {\n  private static readonly MY_SPECIAL_NUMBER = 5;\n\n  bar() {\n    return 2 * Foo.MY_SPECIAL_NUMBER;\n  }\n}\n\n\n```\n\nIf a value can be instantiated more than once over the lifetime of the program, or if users mutate it in any way, it must use `lowerCamelCase`.\nIf a value is an arrow function that implements an interface, then it can be declared `lowerCamelCase`.\n  * **React Components** : When required by JSX, components must be `UpperCamelCase` no matter how the component is implemented (e.g. function, es6 class, `React.createClass`).\n```\n\n\nfunction GoLink(props: GoLinkProps) {\n  return <a href={...}>...;\n}\n\nclass MyDialog extends React.Component {\n  ...\n}\n\n\n```\n```\n\n\n// Lowercased functions cannot be used as JSX elements because the\n// transpiler cannot distinguish them from HTML tags.\nfunction goLink(props: GoLinkProps) {\n  return <a href={...}>...;\n}\n\n\n```\n\n\n\n#### Aliases[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#aliases)\nWhen creating a local-scope alias of an existing symbol, use the format of the existing identifier. The local alias must match the existing naming and format of the source. For variables use `const` for your local aliases, and for class fields use the `readonly` attribute.\n```\n\n\nconst { Foo } = SomeType;\nconst CAPACITY = 5;\n\nclass Teapot {\n  readonly BrewStateEnum = BrewStateEnum;\n  readonly CAPACITY = CAPACITY;\n}\n\n\n```\n\n#### Naming style[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#naming-style)\nTypeScript expresses information in types, so names _should not_ be decorated with information that is included in the type. (See also [Testing Blog](https://testing.googleblog.com/2017/10/code-health-identifiernamingpostforworl.html) for more about what not to include.)\nSome concrete examples of this rule:\n  * Do not use trailing or leading underscores for private properties or methods.\n  * Do not use the `opt_` prefix for optional parameters.\n    * For accessors, see [accessor rules](https://ts.dev/style/#getters-and-setters-accessors) below.\n  * Do not mark interfaces specially (~~`IMyInterface`~~or~~`MyFooInterface`~~) unless it's idiomatic in its environment. When introducing an interface for a class, give it a name that expresses why the interface exists in the first place (e.g.`class TodoItem` and `interface TodoItemStorage` if the interface expresses the format used for storage/serialization in JSON).\n  * Suffixing `Observable`s with `$` is a common external convention and can help resolve confusion regarding observable values vs concrete values. Judgement on whether this is a useful convention is left up to individual teams, but _should_ be consistent within projects.\n\n\n#### Descriptive names[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#descriptive-names)\nNames _must_ be descriptive and clear to a new reader. Do not use abbreviations that are ambiguous or unfamiliar to readers outside your project, and do not abbreviate by deleting letters within a word.\n  * **Exception** : Variables that are in scope for 10 lines or fewer, including arguments that are _not_ part of an exported API, _may_ use short (e.g. single letter) variable names.\n\n\n### File encoding: UTF-8[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#file-encoding-utf-8)\nFor non-ASCII characters, use the actual Unicode character (e.g. `\u221e`). For non-printable characters, the equivalent hex or Unicode escapes (e.g. `\\u221e`) can be used along with an explanatory comment.\n```\n\n\n// Perfectly clear, even without a comment.\nconst units = \"\u03bcs\";\n\n// Use escapes for non-printable characters.\nconst output = \"\\ufeff\" + content; // byte order mark\n\n\n```\n```\n\n\n// Hard to read and prone to mistakes, even with the comment.\nconst units = \"\\u03bcs\"; // Greek letter mu, 's'\n\n// The reader has no idea what this is.\nconst output = \"\\ufeff\" + content;\n\n\n```\n\n### Source Code Formatting[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#source-code-formatting)\nSource code formatting can be completely automated. Humans should not waste time arguing about comma placement in code reviews.\nIn very rare situations (e.g. long multiline container literals or formatting bugs that cause semantic issues), it can be necessary to disable formatter for a section. Use this only where absolutely needed.\n### Comments & Documentation[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#comments-documentation)\n#### JSDoc vs comments[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#jsdoc-vs-comments)\nThere are two types of comments, JSDoc (`/** ... */`) and non-JSDoc ordinary comments (`// ...` or `/* ... */`).\n  * Use `/** JSDoc */` comments for documentation, i.e. comments a user of the code should read.\n  * Use `// line comments` for implementation comments, i.e. comments that only concern the implementation of the code itself.\n\n\nJSDoc comments are understood by tools (such as editors and documentation generators), while ordinary comments are only for other humans.\n#### JSDoc rules follow the JavaScript style[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#jsdoc-rules-follow-the-javascript-style)\nIn general, follow the [JavaScript style guide's rules for JSDoc](https://google.github.io/styleguide/jsguide.html#jsdoc), sections 7.1 - 7.5. The remainder of this section describes exceptions to those rules.\n#### Document all top-level exports of modules[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#document-all-top-level-exports-of-modules)\nUse `/** JSDoc */` comments to communicate information to the users of your code. Avoid merely restating the property or parameter name. You _should_ also document all properties and methods (exported/public or not) whose purpose is not immediately obvious from their name, as judged by your reviewer.\nException: Symbols that are only exported to be consumed by tooling, such as @NgModule classes, do not require comments.\n#### Omit comments that are redundant with TypeScript[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#omit-comments-that-are-redundant-with-typescript)\nFor example, do not declare types in `@param` or `@return` blocks, do not write `@implements`, `@enum`, `@private` etc. on code that uses the `implements`, `enum`, `private` etc. keywords.\n#### Do not use `@override`[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#do-not-use-override)\nDo not use `@override` in TypeScript source code.\n`@override` is not enforced by the compiler, which is surprising and leads to annotations and implementation going out of sync. Including it purely for documentation purposes is confusing.\n#### Make comments that actually add information[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#redundant-comments)\nFor non-exported symbols, sometimes the name and type of the function or parameter is enough. Code will _usually_ benefit from more documentation than just variable names though!\n  * Avoid comments that just restate the parameter name and type, e.g.\n```\n\n/** @param fooBarService The Bar service for the Foo application. */\n\n```\n\n  * Because of this rule, `@param` and `@return` lines are only required when they add information, and may otherwise be omitted.\n```\n\n\n/**\n * POSTs the request to start coffee brewing.\n * @param amountLitres The amount to brew. Must fit the pot size!\n */\nbrew(amountLitres: number, logger: Logger) {\n  // ...\n}\n\n\n```\n\n\n\n#### Parameter property comments[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#parameter-property-comments)\nA parameter property is when a class declares a field and a constructor parameter in a single declaration, by marking a parameter in the constructor. E.g. `constructor(private readonly foo: Foo)`, declares that the class has a `foo` field.\nTo document these fields, use JSDoc's `@param` annotation. Editors display the description on constructor calls and property accesses.\n```\n\n\n/** This class demonstrates how parameter properties are documented. */\nclass ParamProps {\n  /**\n   * @param percolator The percolator used for brewing.\n   * @param beans The beans to brew.\n   */\n  constructor(\n    private readonly percolator: Percolator,\n    private readonly beans: CoffeeBean[]\n  ) {}\n}\n\n\n```\n```\n\n\n/** This class demonstrates how ordinary fields are documented. */\nclass OrdinaryClass {\n  /** The bean that will be used in the next call to brew(). */\n  nextBean: CoffeeBean;\n\n  constructor(initialBean: CoffeeBean) {\n    this.nextBean = initialBean;\n  }\n}\n\n\n```\n\n#### Comments when calling a function[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#comments-when-calling-a-function)\nIf needed, document parameters at call sites inline using block comments. Also consider named parameters using object literals and destructuring. The exact formatting and placement of the comment is not prescribed.\n```\n\n\n// Inline block comments for parameters that'd be hard to understand:\nnew Percolator().brew(/* amountLitres= */ 5);\n// Also consider using named arguments and destructuring parameters (in brew's declaration):\nnew Percolator().brew({ amountLitres: 5 });\n\n\n```\n```\n\n\n/** An ancient {@link CoffeeBrewer} */\nexport class Percolator implements CoffeeBrewer {\n  /**\n   * Brews coffee.\n   * @param amountLitres The amount to brew. Must fit the pot size!\n   */\n  brew(amountLitres: number) {\n    // This implementation creates terrible coffee, but whatever.\n    // TODO(b/12345): Improve percolator brewing.\n  }\n}\n\n\n```\n\n#### Place documentation prior to decorators[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#place-documentation-prior-to-decorators)\nWhen a class, method, or property have both decorators like `@Component` and JsDoc, please make sure to write the JsDoc before the decorator.\n  * Do not write JsDoc between the Decorator and the decorated statement.\n```\n\n\n@Component({\n  selector: \"foo\",\n  template: \"bar\",\n})\n/** Component that prints \"bar\". */\nexport class FooComponent {}\n\n\n```\n\n  * Write the JsDoc block before the Decorator.\n```\n\n\n/** Component that prints \"bar\". */\n@Component({\n  selector: \"foo\",\n  template: \"bar\",\n})\nexport class FooComponent {}\n\n\n```\n\n\n\n## Language Rules[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#language-rules)\n### Visibility[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#visibility)\nRestricting visibility of properties, methods, and entire types helps with keeping code decoupled.\n  * Limit symbol visibility as much as possible.\n  * Consider converting private methods to non-exported functions within the same file but outside of any class, and moving private properties into a separate, non-exported class.\n  * TypeScript symbols are public by default. Never use the `public` modifier except when declaring non-readonly public parameter properties (in constructors).\n\n```\n\n\nclass Foo {\n  public bar = new Bar(); // BAD: public modifier not needed\n\n  constructor(public readonly baz: Baz) {} // BAD: readonly implies it's a property which defaults to public\n}\n\n\n```\n```\n\n\nclass Foo {\n  bar = new Bar(); // GOOD: public modifier not needed\n\n  constructor(public baz: Baz) {} // public modifier allowed\n}\n\n\n```\n\nSee also [export visibility](https://ts.dev/style/#export-visibility) below.\n### Constructors[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#constructors)\nConstructor calls must use parentheses, even when no arguments are passed:\n```\n\n\nconst x = new Foo;\n\n\n```\n```\n\n\nconst x = new Foo();\n\n\n```\n\nIt is unnecessary to provide an empty constructor or one that simply delegates into its parent class because ES2015 provides a default class constructor if one is not specified. However constructors with parameter properties, modifiers or parameter decorators should not be omitted even if the body of the constructor is empty.\n```\n\n\nclass UnnecessaryConstructor {\n  constructor() {}\n}\n\n\n```\n```\n\n\nclass UnnecessaryConstructorOverride extends Base {\n  constructor(value: number) {\n    super(value);\n  }\n}\n\n\n```\n```\n\n\nclass DefaultConstructor {}\n\nclass ParameterProperties {\n  constructor(private myService) {}\n}\n\nclass ParameterDecorators {\n  constructor(@SideEffectDecorator myService) {}\n}\n\nclass NoInstantiation {\n  private constructor() {}\n}\n\n\n```\n\n### Class Members[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#class-members)\n#### No `#private` fields[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#private-fields)\nDo not use private fields (also known as private identifiers):\n```\n\n\nclass Clazz {\n  #ident = 1;\n}\n\n\n```\n\nInstead, use TypeScript's visibility annotations:\n```\n\n\nclass Clazz {\n  private ident = 1;\n}\n\n\n```\nWhy?\nPrivate identifiers cause substantial emit size and performance regressions when down-leveled by TypeScript, and are unsupported before ES2015. At the same time, they do not offer substantial benefits when static type checking is used to enforce visibility.\n#### Use `readonly`[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#use-readonly)\nMark properties that are never reassigned outside of the constructor with the `readonly` modifier (these need not be deeply immutable).\n#### Parameter properties[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#parameter-properties)\nRather than plumbing an obvious initializer through to a class member, use a TypeScript [parameter property](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/classes.html#parameter-properties).\n```\n\n\nclass Foo {\n  private readonly barService: BarService;\n\n  constructor(barService: BarService) {\n    this.barService = barService;\n  }\n}\n\n\n```\n```\n\n\nclass Foo {\n  constructor(private readonly barService: BarService) {}\n}\n\n\n```\n\nIf the parameter property needs documentation, [use an `@param` JSDoc tag](https://ts.dev/style/#parameter-property-comments).\n#### Field initializers[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#field-initializers)\nIf a class member is not a parameter, initialize it where it's declared, which sometimes lets you drop the constructor entirely.\n```\n\n\nclass Foo {\n  private readonly userList: string[];\n  constructor() {\n    this.userList = [];\n  }\n}\n\n\n```\n```\n\n\nclass Foo {\n  private readonly userList: string[] = [];\n}\n\n\n```\n\n#### Properties used outside of class lexical scope[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#properties-used-outside-of-class-lexical-scope)\nProperties used from outside the lexical scope of their containing class, such as an AngularJS controller's properties used from a template, must not use `private` visibility, as they are used outside of the lexical scope of their containing class.\nPrefer `public` visibility for these properties, however `protected` visibility can also be used as needed. For example, Angular and Polymer template properties should use `public`, but AngularJS should use `protected`.\n#### Getters and Setters (Accessors)[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#getters-and-setters-accessors)\n> Warning: Getters and setters have to be transpiled into bloated code. It is a good practice to avoid using getters and setters in general.\nGetters and setters for class members may be used. The getter method must be a [pure function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pure_function) (i.e., result is consistent and has no side effects). They are also useful as a means of restricting the visibility of internal or verbose implementation details (shown below).\n```\n\n\nclass Foo {\n  constructor(private readonly someService: SomeService) {}\n\n  get someMember(): string {\n    return this.someService.someVariable;\n  }\n\n  set someMember(newValue: string) {\n    this.someService.someVariable = newValue;\n  }\n}\n\n\n```\n\nIf an accessor is used to hide a class property, the hidden property may be prefixed or suffixed with any whole word, like `internal` or `wrapped`. When using these private properties, access the value through the accessor whenever possible. At least one accessor for a property must be non-trivial: do not define \"pass-through\" accessors only for the purpose of hiding a property. Instead, make the property public (or consider making it `readonly` rather than just defining a getter with no setter).\n```\n\n\nclass Foo {\n  private wrappedBar = \"\";\n  get bar() {\n    return this.wrappedBar || \"bar\";\n  }\n\n  set bar(wrapped: string) {\n    this.wrappedBar = wrapped.trim();\n  }\n}\n\n\n```\n```\n\n\nclass Bar {\n  private barInternal = \"\";\n  // Neither of these accessors have logic, so just make bar public.\n  get bar() {\n    return this.barInternal;\n  }\n\n  set bar(value: string) {\n    this.barInternal = value;\n  }\n}\n\n\n```\n\n### Primitive Types & Wrapper Classes[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#primitive-types-wrapper-classes)\nTypeScript code must not instantiate the wrapper classes for the primitive types `String`, `Boolean`, and `Number`. Wrapper classes have surprising behaviour, such as `new Boolean(false)` evaluating to `true`.\n```\n\n\nconst s = new String(\"hello\");\nconst b = new Boolean(false);\nconst n = new Number(5);\n\n\n```\n```\n\n\nconst s = \"hello\";\nconst b = false;\nconst n = 5;\n\n\n```\n\n### Array constructor[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#array-constructor)\nTypeScript code must not use the `Array()` constructor, with or without `new`. It has confusing and contradictory usage:\n```\n\n\nconst a = new Array(2); // [undefined, undefined]\nconst b = new Array(2, 3); // [2, 3];\n\n\n```\n\nInstead, always use bracket notation to initialize arrays, or `from` to initialize an `Array` with a certain size:\n```\n\n\nconst a = [2];\nconst b = [2, 3];\n\n// Equivalent to Array(2):\nconst c = [];\nc.length = 2;\n\n// [0, 0, 0, 0, 0]\nArray.from<number>({ length: 5 }).fill(0);\n\n\n```\n\n### Type coercion[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#type-coercion)\nTypeScript code may use the `String()` and `Boolean()` (note: no `new`!) functions, string template literals, or `!!` to coerce types.\n```\n\n\nconst bool = Boolean(false);\nconst str = String(aNumber);\nconst bool2 = !!str;\nconst str2 = `result: ${bool2}`;\n\n\n```\n\nUsing string concatenation to cast to string is discouraged, as we check that operands to the plus operator are of matching types.\nCode must use `Number()` to parse numeric values, and _must_ check its return for `NaN` values explicitly, unless failing to parse is impossible from context.\nNote: `Number('')`, `Number(' ')`, and `Number('\\t')` would return `0` instead of `NaN`. `Number('Infinity')` and `Number('-Infinity')` would return `Infinity` and `-Infinity` respectively. These cases may require special handling.\n```\n\n\nconst aNumber = Number('123');\nif (isNaN(aNumber)) throw new Error(...);  // Handle NaN if the string might not contain a number\nassertFinite(aNumber, ...);                // Optional: if NaN cannot happen because it was validated before.\n\n\n```\n\nCode must not use unary plus (`+`) to coerce strings to numbers. Parsing numbers can fail, has surprising corner cases, and can be a code smell (parsing at the wrong layer). A unary plus is too easy to miss in code reviews given this.\n```\n\n\nconst x = +y;\n\n\n```\n\nCode must also not use `parseInt` or `parseFloat` to parse numbers, except for non-base-10 strings (see below). Both of those functions ignore trailing characters in the string, which can shadow error conditions (e.g. parsing `12 dwarves` as `12`).\n```\n\n\nconst n = parseInt(someString, 10); // Error prone,\nconst f = parseFloat(someString); // regardless of passing a radix.\n\n\n```\n\nCode that must parse using a radix _must_ check that its input is a number before calling into `parseInt`;\n```\n\n\nif (!/^[a-fA-F0-9]+$/.test(someString)) throw new Error(...);\n// Needed to parse hexadecimal.\n// tslint:disable-next-line:ban\nconst n = parseInt(someString, 16);  // Only allowed for radix != 10\n\n\n```\n\nUse `Number()` followed by `Math.floor` or `Math.trunc` (where available) to parse integer numbers:\n```\n\n\nlet f = Number(someString);\nif (isNaN(f)) handleError();\nf = Math.floor(f);\n\n\n```\n\nDo not use explicit boolean coercions in conditional clauses that have implicit boolean coercion. Those are the conditions in an `if`, `for` and `while` statements.\n```\n\n\nconst foo: MyInterface|null = ...;\nif (!!foo) {...}\nwhile (!!foo) {...}\n\n\n```\n```\n\n\nconst foo: MyInterface|null = ...;\nif (foo) {...}\nwhile (foo) {...}\n\n\n```\n\nCode may use explicit comparisons:\n```\n\n\n// Explicitly comparing > 0 is OK:\nif (arr.length > 0) {...}\n// so is relying on boolean coercion:\nif (arr.length) {...}\n\n\n```\n\n### Variables[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#variables)\nAlways use `const` or `let` to declare variables. Use `const` by default, unless a variable needs to be reassigned.
