This also supports principle 3.\nIf it really just doesn't matter that much -- if it's an obscure corner of the language or if it avoids a bug that is unlikely to occur -- it's probably worth leaving out.\n\n\n### Submitting Changes[![copy link to the clipboard](https://ts.dev/svg/link.svg)](https://ts.dev/style/#changes-discuss)\nStill interested? Changes to the style guide are led by the community, finding the consensus of those interested, however the final decision is made by the maintainers of the repository.\n  1. Create a PR changing the style guide with your proposed language.\n  2. Send a PR for <https://github.com/ts-dev-inc/ts.dev/blob/main/style.md>.\n     * If there have been previous discussions of the issue here, include links to those, summarize what they said, and describe why it's worth revisiting.\n     * Ideally, that discussion will include samples.\n  3. A preview URL is generated for PR. Make sure that the Style Guide still looks fine.\n  4. When it seems consensus is reached, it will be approved and become part of the guide after submitting.\n\n\n[![TS.dev logo](https://ts.dev/svg/logo.svg)](https://ts.dev/)\n[Services](https://ts.dev/#services) [Technologies](https://ts.dev/#technologies) [Contacts](https://ts.dev/#contacts) [TS Style Guide](https://ts.dev/style/)\nAll Rights Reserved \u00a9 2021, TS.dev\nAll Rights Reserved \u00a9 2021, TS.dev\n[![GitHub Builds](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/nightly-builds.html)\n\n\n# Do's and Don'ts\n##  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#general-types)General Types\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#number-string-boolean-symbol-and-object)`Number`, `String`, `Boolean`, `Symbol` and `Object`\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** ever use the types `Number`, `String`, `Boolean`, `Symbol`, or `Object` These types refer to non-primitive boxed objects that are almost never used appropriately in JavaScript code.\n```\n\nts\n/* WRONG */\n\n\n\nfunction reverse(s: String): String;\n\n\n```\n\n\u2705 **Do** use the types `number`, `string`, `boolean`, and `symbol`.\n```\n\nts\n/* OK */\n\n\n\nfunction reverse(s: string): string;\n\n\n```\n\nInstead of `Object`, use the non-primitive `object` type ([added in TypeScript 2.2](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/release-notes/typescript-2-2.html#object-type)).\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#generics)Generics\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** ever have a generic type which doesn\u2019t use its type parameter. See more details in [TypeScript FAQ page](https://github.com/Microsoft/TypeScript/wiki/FAQ#why-doesnt-type-inference-work-on-this-interface-interface-foot--).\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#any)any\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** use `any` as a type unless you are in the process of migrating a JavaScript project to TypeScript. The compiler _effectively_ treats `any` as \u201cplease turn off type checking for this thing\u201d. It is similar to putting an `@ts-ignore` comment around every usage of the variable. This can be very helpful when you are first migrating a JavaScript project to TypeScript as you can set the type for stuff you haven\u2019t migrated yet as `any`, but in a full TypeScript project you are disabling type checking for any parts of your program that use it.\nIn cases where you don\u2019t know what type you want to accept, or when you want to accept anything because you will be blindly passing it through without interacting with it, you can use [`unknown`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/#example/unknown-and-never).\n##  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#callback-types)Callback Types\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#return-types-of-callbacks)Return Types of Callbacks\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** use the return type `any` for callbacks whose value will be ignored:\n```\n\nts\n/* WRONG */\n\n\n\nfunction fn(x: () => any) {\n\n\n\n\n  x();\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n\u2705 **Do** use the return type `void` for callbacks whose value will be ignored:\n```\n\nts\n/* OK */\n\n\n\nfunction fn(x: () => void) {\n\n\n\n\n  x();\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n\u2754 **Why:** Using `void` is safer because it prevents you from accidentally using the return value of `x` in an unchecked way:\n```\n\nts\n\nfunction fn(x: () => void) {\n\n\n\n\n  var k = x(); // oops! meant to do something else\n\n\n\n\n  k.doSomething(); // error, but would be OK if the return type had been 'any'\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#optional-parameters-in-callbacks)Optional Parameters in Callbacks\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** use optional parameters in callbacks unless you really mean it:\n```\n\nts\n/* WRONG */\n\n\n\ninterface Fetcher {\n\n\n\n\n  getObject(done: (data: unknown, elapsedTime?: number) => void): void;\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\nThis has a very specific meaning: the `done` callback might be invoked with 1 argument or might be invoked with 2 arguments. The author probably intended to say that the callback might not care about the `elapsedTime` parameter, but there\u2019s no need to make the parameter optional to accomplish this \u2014 it\u2019s always legal to provide a callback that accepts fewer arguments.\n\u2705 **Do** write callback parameters as non-optional:\n```\n\nts\n/* OK */\n\n\n\ninterface Fetcher {\n\n\n\n\n  getObject(done: (data: unknown, elapsedTime: number) => void): void;\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#overloads-and-callbacks)Overloads and Callbacks\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** write separate overloads that differ only on callback arity:\n```\n\nts\n/* WRONG */\n\n\n\ndeclare function beforeAll(action: () => void, timeout?: number): void;\n\n\n\n\ndeclare function beforeAll(\n\n\n\n\n  action: (done: DoneFn) => void,\n\n\n\n\n  timeout?: number\n\n\n\n\n): void;\n\n\n```\n\n\u2705 **Do** write a single overload using the maximum arity:\n```\n\nts\n/* OK */\n\n\n\ndeclare function beforeAll(\n\n\n\n\n  action: (done: DoneFn) => void,\n\n\n\n\n  timeout?: number\n\n\n\n\n): void;\n\n\n```\n\n\u2754 **Why:** It\u2019s always legal for a callback to disregard a parameter, so there\u2019s no need for the shorter overload. Providing a shorter callback first allows incorrectly-typed functions to be passed in because they match the first overload.\n##  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#function-overloads)Function Overloads\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#ordering)Ordering\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** put more general overloads before more specific overloads:\n```\n\nts\n/* WRONG */\n\n\n\ndeclare function fn(x: unknown): unknown;\n\n\n\n\ndeclare function fn(x: HTMLElement): number;\n\n\n\n\ndeclare function fn(x: HTMLDivElement): string;\n\n\n\n\n\n\nvar myElem: HTMLDivElement;\n\n\n\n\nvar x = fn(myElem); // x: unknown, wat?\n\n\n```\n\n\u2705 **Do** sort overloads by putting the more general signatures after more specific signatures:\n```\n\nts\n/* OK */\n\n\n\ndeclare function fn(x: HTMLDivElement): string;\n\n\n\n\ndeclare function fn(x: HTMLElement): number;\n\n\n\n\ndeclare function fn(x: unknown): unknown;\n\n\n\n\n\n\nvar myElem: HTMLDivElement;\n\n\n\n\nvar x = fn(myElem); // x: string, :)\n\n\n```\n\n\u2754 **Why:** TypeScript chooses the _first matching overload_ when resolving function calls. When an earlier overload is \u201cmore general\u201d than a later one, the later one is effectively hidden and cannot be called.\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#use-optional-parameters)Use Optional Parameters\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** write several overloads that differ only in trailing parameters:\n```\n\nts\n/* WRONG */\n\n\n\ninterface Example {\n\n\n\n\n  diff(one: string): number;\n\n\n\n\n  diff(one: string, two: string): number;\n\n\n\n\n  diff(one: string, two: string, three: boolean): number;\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n\u2705 **Do** use optional parameters whenever possible:\n```\n\nts\n/* OK */\n\n\n\ninterface Example {\n\n\n\n\n  diff(one: string, two?: string, three?: boolean): number;\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\nNote that this collapsing should only occur when all overloads have the same return type.\n\u2754 **Why:** This is important for two reasons.\nTypeScript resolves signature compatibility by seeing if any signature of the target can be invoked with the arguments of the source, _and extraneous arguments are allowed_. This code, for example, exposes a bug only when the signature is correctly written using optional parameters:\n```\n\nts\n\nfunction fn(x: (a: string, b: number, c: number) => void) {}\n\n\n\n\nvar x: Example;\n\n\n\n// When written with overloads, OK -- used first overload\n\n\n// When written with optionals, correctly an error\n\n\n\nfn(x.diff);\n\n\n```\n\nThe second reason is when a consumer uses the \u201cstrict null checking\u201d feature of TypeScript. Because unspecified parameters appear as `undefined` in JavaScript, it\u2019s usually fine to pass an explicit `undefined` to a function with optional arguments. This code, for example, should be OK under strict nulls:\n```\n\nts\n\nvar x: Example;\n\n\n\n// When written with overloads, incorrectly an error because of passing 'undefined' to 'string'\n\n\n// When written with optionals, correctly OK\n\n\n\nx.diff(\"something\", true ? undefined : \"hour\");\n\n\n```\n\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#use-union-types)Use Union Types\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** write overloads that differ by type in only one argument position:\n```\n\nts\n/* WRONG */\n\n\n\ninterface Moment {\n\n\n\n\n  utcOffset(): number;\n\n\n\n\n  utcOffset(b: number): Moment;\n\n\n\n\n  utcOffset(b: string): Moment;\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n\u2705 **Do** use union types whenever possible:\n```\n\nts\n/* OK */\n\n\n\ninterface Moment {\n\n\n\n\n  utcOffset(): number;\n\n\n\n\n  utcOffset(b: number | string): Moment;\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\nNote that we didn\u2019t make `b` optional here because the return types of the signatures differ.\n\u2754 **Why:** This is important for people who are \u201cpassing through\u201d a value to your function:\n```\n\nts\n\nfunction fn(x: string): Moment;\n\n\n\n\nfunction fn(x: number): Moment;\n\n\n\n\nfunction fn(x: number | string) {\n\n\n\n\n  // When written with separate overloads, incorrectly an error\n\n\n\n\n  // When written with union types, correctly OK\n\n\n\n\n  return moment().utcOffset(x);\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n##### On this page\n  * [General Types](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#general-types)\n    * [Number, String, Boolean, Symbol and Object](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#number-string-boolean-symbol-and-object)\n    * [Generics](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#generics)\n    * [any](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#any)\n  * [Callback Types](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#callback-types)\n    * [Return Types of Callbacks](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#return-types-of-callbacks)\n    * [Optional Parameters in Callbacks](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#optional-parameters-in-callbacks)\n    * [Overloads and Callbacks](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#overloads-and-callbacks)\n  * [Function Overloads](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#function-overloads)\n    * [Ordering](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#ordering)\n    * [Use Optional Parameters](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#use-optional-parameters)\n    * [Use Union Types](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#use-union-types)\n\n\n##### Is this page helpful?\nYesNo\n[ NextDeep Dive How do d.ts files work, a deep dive Don'ts](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html)\n    * [Deep Dive](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/deep-dive.html)\n    * [Publishing](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/publishing.html)\n    * [Consumption](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/consumption.html)\n  * JavaScript\n    * [JS Projects Utilizing TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro-to-js-ts.html)\n    * [Type Checking JavaScript Files](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/type-checking-javascript-files.html)\n    * [JSDoc Reference](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/jsdoc-supported-types.html)\n    * [Creating .d.ts Files from .js files](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/dts-from-js.html)\n  * Project Configuration\n    * [What is a tsconfig.json](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/tsconfig-json.html)\n    * [Compiler Options in MSBuild](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/compiler-options-in-msbuild.html)\n    * [TSConfig Reference](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/)\n    * [tsc CLI Options](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/compiler-options.html)\n    * [Project References](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/project-references.html)\n    * [Integrating with Build Tools](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/integrating-with-build-tools.html)\n    * [Configuring Watch](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/configuring-watch.html)\n    * [Nightly Builds](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/nightly-builds.html)\n\n\n# Do's and Don'ts\n##  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#general-types)General Types\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#number-string-boolean-symbol-and-object)`Number`, `String`, `Boolean`, `Symbol` and `Object`\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** ever use the types `Number`, `String`, `Boolean`, `Symbol`, or `Object` These types refer to non-primitive boxed objects that are almost never used appropriately in JavaScript code.\n```\n\nts\n/* WRONG */\n\n\n\nfunction reverse(s: String): String;\n\n\n```\n\n\u2705 **Do** use the types `number`, `string`, `boolean`, and `symbol`.\n```\n\nts\n/* OK */\n\n\n\nfunction reverse(s: string): string;\n\n\n```\n\nInstead of `Object`, use the non-primitive `object` type ([added in TypeScript 2.2](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/release-notes/typescript-2-2.html#object-type)).\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#generics)Generics\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** ever have a generic type which doesn\u2019t use its type parameter. See more details in [TypeScript FAQ page](https://github.com/Microsoft/TypeScript/wiki/FAQ#why-doesnt-type-inference-work-on-this-interface-interface-foot--).\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#any)any\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** use `any` as a type unless you are in the process of migrating a JavaScript project to TypeScript. The compiler _effectively_ treats `any` as \u201cplease turn off type checking for this thing\u201d. It is similar to putting an `@ts-ignore` comment around every usage of the variable. This can be very helpful when you are first migrating a JavaScript project to TypeScript as you can set the type for stuff you haven\u2019t migrated yet as `any`, but in a full TypeScript project you are disabling type checking for any parts of your program that use it.\nIn cases where you don\u2019t know what type you want to accept, or when you want to accept anything because you will be blindly passing it through without interacting with it, you can use [`unknown`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/#example/unknown-and-never).\n##  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#callback-types)Callback Types\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#return-types-of-callbacks)Return Types of Callbacks\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** use the return type `any` for callbacks whose value will be ignored:\n```\n\nts\n/* WRONG */\n\n\n\nfunction fn(x: () => any) {\n\n\n\n\n  x();\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n\u2705 **Do** use the return type `void` for callbacks whose value will be ignored:\n```\n\nts\n/* OK */\n\n\n\nfunction fn(x: () => void) {\n\n\n\n\n  x();\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n\u2754 **Why:** Using `void` is safer because it prevents you from accidentally using the return value of `x` in an unchecked way:\n```\n\nts\n\nfunction fn(x: () => void) {\n\n\n\n\n  var k = x(); // oops! meant to do something else\n\n\n\n\n  k.doSomething(); // error, but would be OK if the return type had been 'any'\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#optional-parameters-in-callbacks)Optional Parameters in Callbacks\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** use optional parameters in callbacks unless you really mean it:\n```\n\nts\n/* WRONG */\n\n\n\ninterface Fetcher {\n\n\n\n\n  getObject(done: (data: unknown, elapsedTime?: number) => void): void;\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\nThis has a very specific meaning: the `done` callback might be invoked with 1 argument or might be invoked with 2 arguments. The author probably intended to say that the callback might not care about the `elapsedTime` parameter, but there\u2019s no need to make the parameter optional to accomplish this \u2014 it\u2019s always legal to provide a callback that accepts fewer arguments.\n\u2705 **Do** write callback parameters as non-optional:\n```\n\nts\n/* OK */\n\n\n\ninterface Fetcher {\n\n\n\n\n  getObject(done: (data: unknown, elapsedTime: number) => void): void;\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#overloads-and-callbacks)Overloads and Callbacks\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** write separate overloads that differ only on callback arity:\n```\n\nts\n/* WRONG */\n\n\n\ndeclare function beforeAll(action: () => void, timeout?: number): void;\n\n\n\n\ndeclare function beforeAll(\n\n\n\n\n  action: (done: DoneFn) => void,\n\n\n\n\n  timeout?: number\n\n\n\n\n): void;\n\n\n```\n\n\u2705 **Do** write a single overload using the maximum arity:\n```\n\nts\n/* OK */\n\n\n\ndeclare function beforeAll(\n\n\n\n\n  action: (done: DoneFn) => void,\n\n\n\n\n  timeout?: number\n\n\n\n\n): void;\n\n\n```\n\n\u2754 **Why:** It\u2019s always legal for a callback to disregard a parameter, so there\u2019s no need for the shorter overload. Providing a shorter callback first allows incorrectly-typed functions to be passed in because they match the first overload.\n##  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#function-overloads)Function Overloads\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#ordering)Ordering\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** put more general overloads before more specific overloads:\n```\n\nts\n/* WRONG */\n\n\n\ndeclare function fn(x: unknown): unknown;\n\n\n\n\ndeclare function fn(x: HTMLElement): number;\n\n\n\n\ndeclare function fn(x: HTMLDivElement): string;\n\n\n\n\n\n\nvar myElem: HTMLDivElement;\n\n\n\n\nvar x = fn(myElem); // x: unknown, wat?\n\n\n```\n\n\u2705 **Do** sort overloads by putting the more general signatures after more specific signatures:\n```\n\nts\n/* OK */\n\n\n\ndeclare function fn(x: HTMLDivElement): string;\n\n\n\n\ndeclare function fn(x: HTMLElement): number;\n\n\n\n\ndeclare function fn(x: unknown): unknown;\n\n\n\n\n\n\nvar myElem: HTMLDivElement;\n\n\n\n\nvar x = fn(myElem); // x: string, :)\n\n\n```\n\n\u2754 **Why:** TypeScript chooses the _first matching overload_ when resolving function calls. When an earlier overload is \u201cmore general\u201d than a later one, the later one is effectively hidden and cannot be called.\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#use-optional-parameters)Use Optional Parameters\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** write several overloads that differ only in trailing parameters:\n```\n\nts\n/* WRONG */\n\n\n\ninterface Example {\n\n\n\n\n  diff(one: string): number;\n\n\n\n\n  diff(one: string, two: string): number;\n\n\n\n\n  diff(one: string, two: string, three: boolean): number;\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n\u2705 **Do** use optional parameters whenever possible:\n```\n\nts\n/* OK */\n\n\n\ninterface Example {\n\n\n\n\n  diff(one: string, two?: string, three?: boolean): number;\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\nNote that this collapsing should only occur when all overloads have the same return type.\n\u2754 **Why:** This is important for two reasons.\nTypeScript resolves signature compatibility by seeing if any signature of the target can be invoked with the arguments of the source, _and extraneous arguments are allowed_. This code, for example, exposes a bug only when the signature is correctly written using optional parameters:\n```\n\nts\n\nfunction fn(x: (a: string, b: number, c: number) => void) {}\n\n\n\n\nvar x: Example;\n\n\n\n// When written with overloads, OK -- used first overload\n\n\n// When written with optionals, correctly an error\n\n\n\nfn(x.diff);\n\n\n```\n\nThe second reason is when a consumer uses the \u201cstrict null checking\u201d feature of TypeScript. Because unspecified parameters appear as `undefined` in JavaScript, it\u2019s usually fine to pass an explicit `undefined` to a function with optional arguments. This code, for example, should be OK under strict nulls:\n```\n\nts\n\nvar x: Example;\n\n\n\n// When written with overloads, incorrectly an error because of passing 'undefined' to 'string'\n\n\n// When written with optionals, correctly OK\n\n\n\nx.diff(\"something\", true ? undefined : \"hour\");\n\n\n```\n\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#use-union-types)Use Union Types\n\u274c **Don\u2019t** write overloads that differ by type in only one argument position:\n```\n\nts\n/* WRONG */\n\n\n\ninterface Moment {\n\n\n\n\n  utcOffset(): number;\n\n\n\n\n  utcOffset(b: number): Moment;\n\n\n\n\n  utcOffset(b: string): Moment;\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n\u2705 **Do** use union types whenever possible:\n```\n\nts\n/* OK */\n\n\n\ninterface Moment {\n\n\n\n\n  utcOffset(): number;\n\n\n\n\n  utcOffset(b: number | string): Moment;\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\nNote that we didn\u2019t make `b` optional here because the return types of the signatures differ.\n\u2754 **Why:** This is important for people who are \u201cpassing through\u201d a value to your function:\n```\n\nts\n\nfunction fn(x: string): Moment;\n\n\n\n\nfunction fn(x: number): Moment;\n\n\n\n\nfunction fn(x: number | string) {\n\n\n\n\n  // When written with separate overloads, incorrectly an error\n\n\n\n\n  // When written with union types, correctly OK\n\n\n\n\n  return moment().utcOffset(x);\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n##### On this page\n  * [General Types](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#general-types)\n    * [Number, String, Boolean, Symbol and Object](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#number-string-boolean-symbol-and-object)\n    * [Generics](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#generics)\n    * [any](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#any)\n  * [Callback Types](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#callback-types)\n    * [Return Types of Callbacks](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#return-types-of-callbacks)\n    * [Optional Parameters in Callbacks](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#optional-parameters-in-callbacks)\n    * [Overloads and Callbacks](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#overloads-and-callbacks)\n  * [Function Overloads](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#function-overloads)\n    * [Ordering](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#ordering)\n    * [Use Optional Parameters](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#use-optional-parameters)\n    * [Use Union Types](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/do-s-and-don-ts.html#use-union-types)\n\n\n##### Is this page helpful?\nYesNo\n[ NextDeep Dive How do d.ts files work, a deep dive ](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/declaration-files/deep-dive.html)\nThe TypeScript docs are an open source project. \\(3\\)](https://gravatar.com/avatar/b710592f44be3f43546d5e843d8fbd7122e66102589747658184b7bc398aaea5?s=32&&d=blank)\nMF![Martin Fischer  \\(1\\)](https://gravatar.com/avatar/dc885100e48a5949f1a44bcd6fce398735ff3294f20607ddee8bb45acb29601a?s=32&&d=blank)\nJ![Jongbin minutes](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-in-5-minutes.html)\nAn overview of building a TypeScript web app\n  * [TSConfig Options](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/)\nAll Help](https://www.typescriptlang.org/community)\n  * [Blog](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/typescript/)\n  * [](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/#readme)\n  * [](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript-Website)\n\n\nMSG\n\n\n---\n\n[Skip to main content](https://www.typescriptlang.org/#site-content)\n[TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)\n  * [Download](https://www.typescriptlang.org/download/)\n  * [Docs](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/)\n  * [Handbook](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html)\n  * [Community](https://www.typescriptlang.org/community/)\n  * [Playground](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/)\n  * [Tools](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tools/)\n\n\n[in En](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)\n# TypeScript is **JavaScript with syntax for types.**\nTypeScript is a strongly typed programming language that builds on JavaScript, giving you better tooling at any scale.\n[ Try TypeScript Now Online or via npm ](https://www.typescriptlang.org/download)\n  * [Editor Checks](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)\n  * [Auto-complete](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)\n  * [Interfaces](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)\n  * [JSX](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)\n\n\n```\n\nts\n\nconst user = {\n\n\n\n\n  firstName: \"Angela\",\n\n\n\n\n  lastName: \"Davis\",\n\n\n\n\n  role: \"Professor\",\n\n\n\n}\n\n\n\u00a0\n\n\n\nconsole.log(user.name)\n\n\nProperty 'name' does not exist on type '{ firstName: string; lastName: string; role: string; }'.2339Property 'name' does not exist on type '{ firstName: string; lastName: string; role: string; }'.\n\u00a0\n\n```\n```\n\nts\n\nconst user = {\n\n\n\n\n  firstName: \"Angela\",\n\n\n\n\n  lastName: \"Davis\",\n\n\n\n\n  role: \"Professor\",\n\n\n\n}\n\n\n\u00a0\n\n\n\nconsole.log(user.name)\n\n\nProperty 'name' does not exist on type '{ firstName: string; lastName: string; role: string; }'.2339Property 'name' does not exist on type '{ firstName: string; lastName: string; role: string; }'.\n\u00a0\n\n```\n\n[](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)\n[TypeScript 6.0](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/release-notes/typescript-6-0.html) is now available\n## What is TypeScript?\n### JavaScript and More\nTypeScript adds additional syntax to JavaScript to support a **tighter integration with your editor**. Catch errors early in your editor.\n### A Result You Can Trust\nTypeScript code converts to JavaScript, which **runs anywhere JavaScript runs** : In a browser, on Node.js, Deno, Bun and in your apps.\n### Safety at Scale\nTypeScript understands JavaScript and uses **type inference to give you great tooling** without additional code.\n## Get Started\n[ Handbook Learn the language ](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html)\n[ Playground Try in your browser ](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play)\n[ Download Install TypeScript ](https://www.typescriptlang.org/download/)\n## Adopt TypeScript Gradually\nJavaScript file\nApply types to your JavaScript project incrementally, **each step improves editor support** and improves your codebase.\nLet's take this incorrect JavaScript code, and see how **TypeScript can catch mistakes in your editor**.\n```\n\njs\n\nfunction compact(arr) {\n\n\n\n\n    if (orr.length > 10)\n\n\n\n\n        return arr.trim(0, 10)\n\n\n\n\n    return arr\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\nNo editor warnings in JavaScript files  \n  \nThis code crashes at runtime!\nJavaScript file\n```\n\njs\n// @ts-check\n\n\n\u00a0\n\n\n\nfunction compact(arr) {\n\n\n\n\n  if (orr.length > 10)\n\n\nCannot find name 'orr'.2304Cannot find name 'orr'.\n\n    return arr.trim(0, 10)\n\n\n\n\n  return arr\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\nAdding this to a JS file shows errors in your editor\nthe param is arr, not orr!\nJavaScript with TS Check\n```\n\njs\n// @ts-check\n\n\n\u00a0\n\n\n\n/** @param {any[]} arr */\n\n\n\n\nfunction compact(arr) {\n\n\n\n\n    if (arr.length > 10)\n\n\n\n\n      return arr.trim(0, 10)\n\n\nProperty 'trim' does not exist on type 'any[]'.2339Property 'trim' does not exist on type 'any[]'.\n\n    return arr\n\n\n\n  }\n\n```\n\nUsing JSDoc to give type information\nNow TS has found a bad call. Arrays have slice, not trim.\nJavaScript with JSDoc\n```\n\nts\n\nfunction compact(arr: string[]) {\n\n\n\n\n  if (arr.length > 10)\n\n\n\n\n    return arr.slice(0, 10)\n\n\n\n\n  return arr\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\nTypeScript adds natural syntax for providing types\nTypeScript file\n### Describe Your Data\n**Describe the shape of objects and functions** in your code.\nMaking it possible to see **documentation and issues in your editor**.\n```\n\nts\n\ninterface Account {\n\n\n\n\n  id: number\n\n\n\n\n  displayName: string\n\n\n\n\n  version: 1\n\n\n\n}\n\n\n\u00a0\n\n\n\nfunction welcome(user: Account) {\n\n\n\n\n  console.log(user.id)\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n```\n\nts\n\ntype Result = \"pass\" | \"fail\"\n\n\n\n\u00a0\n\n\n\nfunction verify(result: Result) {\n\n\n\n\n  if (result === \"pass\") {\n\n\n\n\n    console.log(\"Passed\")\n\n\n\n\n  } else {\n\n\n\n\n    console.log(\"Failed\")\n\n\n\n  }\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n## TypeScript becomes JavaScript via the delete key.\n```\n\nts\n\ntype Result = \"pass\" | \"fail\"\n\n\n\n\u00a0\n\n\n\nfunction verify(result: Result) {\n\n\n\n\n  if (result === \"pass\") {\n\n\n\n\n    console.log(\"Passed\")\n\n\n\n\n  } else {\n\n\n\n\n    console.log(\"Failed\")\n\n\n\n  }\n\n\n}\n\n\n\u00a0\n\n```\n\n**TypeScript file**.\n```\n\nts\n\ntype Result = \"pass\" | \"fail\"\n\n\n\n\u00a0\n\n\n\nfunction verify(result: Result) {\n\n\n\n\n  if (result === \"pass\") {\n\n\n\n\n    console.log(\"Passed\")\n\n\n\n\n  } else {\n\n\n\n\n    console.log(\"Failed\")\n\n\n\n  }\n\n\n}\n\n\n\u00a0\n\n```\n\n**Types are removed**.\n```\n\njs\n\u00a0\n\n\n\u00a0\n\n\n\nfunction verify(result) {\n\n\n\n\n  if (result === \"pass\") {\n\n\n\n\n    console.log(\"Passed\")\n\n\n\n\n  } else {\n\n\n\n\n    console.log(\"Failed\")\n\n\n\n  }\n\n\n}\n\n\n\u00a0\n\n```\n\n**JavaScript file**.\n## TypeScript Testimonials\n**First** , we were surprised by the number of small bugs we found when converting our code.\n**Second** , we underestimated how powerful the editor integration is.\nTypeScript was such a boon to our stability and sanity that we started using it for all new code within days of starting the conversion.\nFelix Rieseberg at Slack covered the transition of their desktop app from JavaScript to TypeScript in their blog\n[Read](https://slack.engineering/typescript-at-slack-a81307fa288d)\n[](https://www.typescriptlang.org/#m-stories)[](https://www.typescriptlang.org/#m-stories)\n### Open Source with TypeScript\n[Angular](https://angular.io/)[Vue](https://vuejs.org/)[Jest](https://jestjs.io/)[Redux](https://redux.js.org/)[Ionic](https://ionicframework.com/)[Probot](https://probot.github.io/)[Deno](https://deno.land/)[Vercel](https://github.com/vercel/vercel)[Yarn](https://yarnpkg.com/)[GitHub  \nDesktop](https://github.com/desktop/desktop/)\n## Loved by Developers\n![Image of the stack overflow logo, and a graph showing TypeScript as the 2nd most popular language](https://www.typescriptlang.org/images/index/stack-overflow.svg)\nVoted **2nd most loved programming language** in the [Stack Overflow 2020 Developer survey](https://insights.stackoverflow.com/survey/2020#most-loved-dreaded-and-wanted)\n![Logo of the State of JS survey](https://www.typescriptlang.org/images/index/state-of-js.svg)\nTypeScript was **used by 78%** of the [2020 State of JS](https://2020.stateofjs.com/en-US/technologies/javascript-flavors/) respondents, with **93% saying they would use it again**.\nTypeScript was given the award for **\u201cMost Adopted Technology\u201d** based on year-on-year growth.\n## Get Started\n[ Handbook Learn the language ](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html)\n[ Playground Try in your browser ](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play)\n[ Download Install TypeScript ](https://www.typescriptlang.org/download/)\nMade with \u2665 in Redmond, Boston, SF & Dublin\n[![Microsoft Logo](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)\n\u00a9 2012-2026 Microsoft  \n[Privacy](https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=521839 \"Microsoft Privacy Policy\")[Terms of Use](https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkID=206977)\n### Using TypeScript\n  * [Get Started](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/)\n  * [Download](https://www.typescriptlang.org/download/)\n  * [Community](https://www.typescriptlang.org/community/)\n  * [Playground](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/)\n  * [TSConfig Ref](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/)\n  * [Code Samples](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/#show-examples)\n  * [Why TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/why-create-typescript/)\n  * [Design](https://www.typescriptlang.org/branding/)\n\n\n### Community\n  * [Get Help](https://www.typescriptlang.org/community)\n  * [Blog](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/typescript/)\n  * [](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/#readme)\n  * [](https://discord.gg/typescript)\n  * [](https://twitter.com/TypeScript)\n  * [](https://fosstodon.org/@TypeScript)\n  * [](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/typescript)\n  * [](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript-Website)\n\n\nMSG\n\n\n---\n\n[Skip to main content](https://www.typescriptlang.org/download/#site-content)\n[TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)\n  * [Download](https://www.typescriptlang.org/download/)\n  * [Docs](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/)\n  * [Handbook](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html)\n  * [Community](https://www.typescriptlang.org/community/)\n  * [Playground](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/)\n  * [Tools](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tools/)\n\n\n[in En](https://www.typescriptlang.org/download/)\n# Download TypeScript\nTypeScript can be installed through three installation routes depending on how you intend to use it: an npm module, a NuGet package or a Visual Studio Extension.\nIf you are using Node.js, you want the npm version. If you are using MSBuild in your project, you want the NuGet package or Visual Studio extension.\n## TypeScript in Your Project\nHaving TypeScript set up on a per-project basis lets you have many projects with many different versions of TypeScript, this keeps each project working consistently.\n### via npm\nTypeScript is available as a [package on the npm registry](https://www.npmjs.com/package/typescript) available as `\"typescript\"`.\nYou will need a copy of [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/en/ \"Link to the node.js project\") as an environment to run the package. Then you use a dependency manager like [npm](https://www.npmjs.com/ \"Link to the npm package manager\"), [yarn](https://yarnpkg.com/ \"Link to the yarn package manager\") or [pnpm](https://pnpm.js.org/ \"Link to the pnpm package manager\") to download TypeScript into your project.\n`npm install typescript --save-dev`  \n  \nnpm yarn pnpm\nAll of these dependency managers support lockfiles, ensuring that everyone on your team is using the same version of the language. You can then run the TypeScript compiler using one of the following commands:\n`npx tsc`  \n  \nnpm yarn pnpm\n### with Visual Studio\nFor most project types, you can get TypeScript as a package in Nuget for your MSBuild projects, for example an ASP.NET Core app.\nWhen using Nuget, you can [install TypeScript through Visual Studio](https://learn.microsoft.com/visualstudio/javascript/tutorial-aspnet-with-typescript) using:\n  * The Manage NuGet Packages window (which you can get to by right-clicking on a project node)\n  * The Nuget Package Manager Console (found in Tools > NuGet Package Manager > Package Manager Console) and then running:  \n`Install-Package Microsoft.TypeScript.MSBuild`\n\n\nFor project types which don't support Nuget, you can use the [ TypeScript Visual Studio extension](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=TypeScriptTeam.typescript-593). You can [install the extension](https://learn.microsoft.com/visualstudio/ide/finding-and-using-visual-studio-extensions) using `Extensions > Manage Extensions` in Visual Studio.\nThe examples below are for more advanced use cases.\n## Globally Installing TypeScript\nIt can be handy to have TypeScript available across all projects, often to test one-off ideas. Long-term, codebases should prefer a project-wide installation over a global install so that they can benefit from reproducible builds across different machines.\n### via npm\nYou can use npm to install TypeScript globally, this means that you can use the `tsc` command anywhere in your terminal.\nTo do this, run `npm install -g typescript`. This will install the latest version (currently 6.0).\n### via Visual Studio Marketplace\nYou can install TypeScript as a Visual Studio extension, which will allow you to use TypeScript across many MSBuild projects in Visual Studio.\nThe latest version is available [in the Visual Studio Marketplace](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=TypeScriptTeam.typescript-593 \"Link to the Visual Studio Marketplace for the TypeScript MSBuild extension\").\n## Working with TypeScript-compatible transpilers\nThere are other tools which convert TypeScript files to JavaScript files. You might use these tools for speed or consistency with your existing build tooling.\nEach of these projects handle the file conversion, but do not handle the type-checking aspects of the TypeScript compiler. So it's likely that you will still need to keep the above TypeScript dependency around, and you will want to enable [`isolatedModules`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#isolatedModules).\n### Babel\n[Babel](https://babeljs.io/) is a very popular JavaScript transpiler which supports TypeScript files via the plugin [@babel/plugin-transform-typescript](https://babeljs.io/docs/en/babel-preset-typescript#docsNav).\n### swc\n[swc](https://swc-project.github.io/docs/installation/) is a fast transpiler created in Rust which supports many of Babel's features including TypeScript.\n### Sucrase\n[Sucrase](https://github.com/alangpierce/sucrase#sucrase/) is a Babel fork focused on speed for using in development mode. Sucrase supports TypeScript natively.\n## Next Steps\n#### Get Started\n  * [JS to TS](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-in-5-minutes.html)\n  * [New to Programming](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html)\n  * [OOP to JS](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-in-5-minutes-oop.html)\n  * [Functional Builds](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/nightly-builds.html)\n\n\n# The TypeScript Handbook\n##  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html#about-this-handbook)About this Handbook\nOver 20 years after its introduction to the programming community, JavaScript is now one of the most widespread cross-platform languages ever created. Starting as a small scripting language for adding trivial interactivity to webpages, JavaScript has grown to be a language of choice for both frontend and backend applications of every size. While the size, scope, and complexity of programs written in JavaScript has grown exponentially, the ability of the JavaScript language to express the relationships between different units of code has not. Combined with JavaScript\u2019s rather peculiar runtime semantics, this mismatch between language and program complexity has made JavaScript development a difficult task to manage at scale.\nThe most common kinds of errors that programmers write can be described as type errors: a certain kind of value was used where a different kind of value was expected. This could be due to simple typos, a failure to understand the API surface of a library, incorrect assumptions about runtime behavior, or other errors. The goal of TypeScript is to be a static typechecker for JavaScript programs - in other words, a tool that runs before your code runs (static) and ensures that the types of the program are correct (typechecked).\nIf you are coming to TypeScript without a JavaScript background, with the intention of TypeScript being your first language, we recommend you first start reading the documentation on either the [Microsoft Learn JavaScript tutorial](https://developer.microsoft.com/javascript/) or read [JavaScript at the Mozilla Web Docs](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide). If you have experience in other languages, you should be able to pick up JavaScript syntax quite quickly by reading the handbook.\n##  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html#how-is-this-handbook-structured)How is this Handbook Structured\nThe handbook is split into two sections:\n  * **The Handbook**\nThe TypeScript Handbook is intended to be a comprehensive document that explains TypeScript to everyday programmers. You can read the handbook by going from top to bottom in the left-hand navigation.\nYou should expect each chapter or page to provide you with a strong understanding of the given concepts. The TypeScript Handbook is not a complete language specification, but it is intended to be a comprehensive guide to all of the language\u2019s features and behaviors.\nA reader who completes the walkthrough should be able to:\n    * Read and understand commonly-used TypeScript syntax and patterns\n    * Explain the effects of important compiler options\n    * Correctly predict type system behavior in most cases\nIn the interests of clarity and brevity, the main content of the Handbook will not explore every edge case or minutiae of the features being covered. You can find more details on particular concepts in the reference articles.\n  * **Reference Files**\nThe reference section below the handbook in the navigation is built to provide a richer understanding of how a particular part of TypeScript works. You can read it top-to-bottom, but each section aims to provide a deeper explanation of a single concept - meaning there is no aim for continuity.\n\n\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html#non-goals)Non-Goals\nThe Handbook is also intended to be a concise document that can be comfortably read in a few hours. Certain topics won\u2019t be covered in order to keep things short.\nSpecifically, the Handbook does not fully introduce core JavaScript basics like functions, classes, and closures. Where appropriate, we\u2019ll include links to background reading that you can use to read up on those concepts.\nThe Handbook also isn\u2019t intended to be a replacement for a language specification. In some cases, edge cases or formal descriptions of behavior will be skipped in favor of high-level, easier-to-understand explanations. Instead, there are separate reference pages that more precisely and formally describe many aspects of TypeScript\u2019s behavior. The reference pages are not intended for readers unfamiliar with TypeScript, so they may use advanced terminology or reference topics you haven\u2019t read about yet.\nFinally, the Handbook won\u2019t cover how TypeScript interacts with other tools, except where necessary. Topics like how to configure TypeScript with webpack, rollup, parcel, react, babel, closure, lerna, rush, bazel, preact, vue, angular, svelte, jquery, yarn, or npm are out of scope - you can find these resources elsewhere on the web.\n##  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html#get-started)Get Started\nBefore getting started with [The Basics](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/basic-types.html), we recommend reading one of the following introductory pages. These introductions are intended to highlight key similarities and differences between TypeScript and your favored programming language, and clear up common misconceptions specific to those languages.\n  * [TypeScript for the New Programmer](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html)\n  * [TypeScript for JavaScript Programmers](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-in-5-minutes.html)\n  * [TypeScript for Java/C# Programmers](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-in-5-minutes-oop.html)\n  * [TypeScript for Functional Programmers](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-in-5-minutes-func.html)\n\n\nOtherwise, jump to [The Basics](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/basic-types.html).\n##### On this page\n  * [About this Handbook](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html#about-this-handbook)\n  * [How is this Handbook Structured](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html#how-is-this-handbook-structured)\n    * [Non-Goals](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html#non-goals)\n  * [Get Started](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html#get-started)\n\n\n##### Is this page helpful?\nYesNo\n[ NextThe Basics Step one in learning TypeScript: The basic types. ](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/basic-types.html)\nThe TypeScript docs are an open source project. En](https://www.typescriptlang.org/community/)\n# Connect with us\n## Online\nTell us what\u2019s working well, what you want to see added or improved, and find out about new updates.\n### [Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/typescript \"TypeScript tag on Stack Overflow\")Engage with your peers and ask questions about TypeScript using the tag 'typescript'\n### [Chat](https://discord.gg/typescript \"TypeScript Community on Discord\")Chat with other TypeScript users in the TypeScript Community Chat.\n### [GitHub](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/new/choose \"Create a new GitHub Issue on the TypeScript repo\")Found a bug, or want to give us constructive feedback? [Tell us on GitHub](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/new/choose \"Create a new GitHub Issue on the TypeScript repo\")\n### [Twitter](https://twitter.com/typescript \"The TypeScript team on Twitter\")Stay up to date. Follow us on Twitter [@typescript](https://twitter.com/typescript \"The TypeScript team on Twitter\")!\n### [Blog](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/typescript/ \"The official TypeScript blog\")Learn about the latest TypeScript developments via our [blog](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/typescript/ \"The official TypeScript blog\")!\n### [Definitely Typed](https://github.com/definitelytyped/definitelytyped/#definitelytyped \"Definitely Typed, a central location for third party type definitions\")TypeScript definition files. [Browse the thousands of](https://github.com/definitelytyped/definitelytyped/#definitelytyped \"Definitely Typed, a central location for third party type definitions\") available for common libraries and frameworks.\n## Connect in person\nConferencesUpcoming EventsMeetups\n### Conferences\n[![logo of  TSConf:EU](https://www.typescriptlang.org/community/\\[object%20Module\\])](https://tsconf.eu should be used in the editor\n  * [Examples ](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/)\nClose\nJavaScriptTypeScript\nSee how TypeScript improves day to day working with JavaScript with minimal additional syntax.\n#### JavaScript Essentials\n    1. [Hello World](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?target=1&q=19#example/hello-world \"Open the example: Hello World\")\n    2. [Objects and Arrays](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?strict=false&q=174#example/objects-and-arrays \"Open the example: Objects and Arrays\")\n    3. [Functions](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?noImplicitAny=false&q=70#example/functions \"Open the example: Functions\")\n    4. [Code Flow](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?strictNullChecks=true&q=231#example/code-flow \"Open the example: Code Flow\")\n#### Functions with JavaScript\n    1. [Generic Functions](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=228#example/generic-functions \"Open the example: Generic Functions\")\n    2. [Typing Functions](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=200#example/typing-functions \"Open the example: Typing Functions\")\n    3. [Function Chaining](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?esModuleInterop=true&q=41#example/function-chaining \"Open the example: Function Chaining\")\n#### Working With Classes\n    1. [Classes 101](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=222#example/classes-101 \"Open the example: Classes 101\")\n    2. [This](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=234#example/this \"Open the example: This\")\n    3. [Generic Classes](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=23#example/generic-classes \"Open the example: Generic Classes\")\n    4. [Mixins](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=58#example/mixins \"Open the example: Mixins\")\n#### Modern JavaScript\n    1. [Async Await](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=446#example/async-await \"Open the example: Async Await\")\n    2. [Immutability](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=120#example/immutability \"Open the example: Immutability\")\n    3. [Import Export](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=198#example/import-export \"Open the example: Import Export\")\n    4. [JSDoc Support](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?useJavaScript=trueq=115#example/jsdoc-support \"Open the example: JSDoc Support\")\n#### External APIs\n    1. [TypeScript with Web](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?useJavaScript=trueq=236#example/typescript-with-web \"Open the example: TypeScript with Web\")\n    2. [TypeScript with React](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?jsx=2&esModuleInterop=true&q=65#example/typescript-with-react \"Open the example: TypeScript with React\")\n    3. [TypeScript with Deno](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=12#example/typescript-with-deno \"Open the example: TypeScript with Deno\")\n    4. [TypeScript with Node](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?useJavaScript=trueq=11#example/typescript-with-node \"Open the example: TypeScript with Node\")\n    5. [TypeScript with WebGL](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?useJavaScript=trueq=320#example/typescript-with-webgl \"Open the example: TypeScript with WebGL\")\n#### Helping with JavaScript\n    1. [Quick Fixes](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=368#example/quick-fixes \"Open the example: Quick Fixes\")\n    2. [Errors](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=291#example/errors \"Open the example: Errors\")\nExplore how TypeScript extends JavaScript to add more safety and tooling.\n#### Primitives\n    1. [Any](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=238#example/any \"Open the example: Any\")\n    2. [Literals](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=384#example/literals \"Open the example: Literals\")\n    3. [Union and Intersection Types](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=469#example/union-and-intersection-types \"Open the example: Union and Intersection Types\")\n    4. [Unknown and Never](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=176#example/unknown-and-never \"Open the example: Unknown and Never\")\n#### Type Primitives\n    1. [Tuples](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/?q=399#example/tuples \"Open the example: Tuples\")\n    2. TypeScript code. Try different compiler flags, run through extensive code samples to learn specifics about how TypeScript works.\n[![](https://www.typescriptlang.org/static/tsconfig-ref-cf26083c052d3495a7f06f12850b28f1.png)TSConfig Reference](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/)\nAn annotated reference to more than a hundred compiler options available in a `tsconfig.json` or `jsconfig.json`.\n[![Preview of the cheat sheets Builds](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/nightly-builds.html)\n\n\n# TypeScript for the New Programmer\nCongratulations on choosing TypeScript as one of your first languages \u2014 you\u2019re already making good decisions!\nYou\u2019ve probably already heard that TypeScript is a \u201cflavor\u201d or \u201cvariant\u201d of JavaScript. The relationship between TypeScript (TS) and JavaScript (JS) is rather unique among modern programming languages, so learning more about this relationship will help you understand how TypeScript adds to JavaScript.\n##  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html#what-is-javascript-a-brief-history)What is JavaScript? A Brief History\nJavaScript (also known as ECMAScript) started its life as a simple scripting language for browsers. At the time it was invented, it was expected to be used for short snippets of code embedded in a web page \u2014 writing more than a few dozen lines of code would have been somewhat unusual. Due to this, early web browsers executed such code pretty slowly. Over time, though, JS became more and more popular, and web developers started using it to create interactive experiences.\nWeb browser developers responded to this increased JS usage by optimizing their execution engines (dynamic compilation) and extending what could be done with it (adding APIs), which in turn made web developers use it even more. On modern websites, your browser is frequently running applications that span hundreds of thousands of lines of code. This is the long and gradual growth of \u201cthe web\u201d, starting as a simple network of static pages, and evolving into a platform for rich _applications_ of all kinds.\nMore than this, JS has become popular enough to be used outside the context of browsers, such as implementing JS servers using node.js. The \u201crun anywhere\u201d nature of JS makes it an attractive choice for cross-platform development. There are many developers these days that use _only_ JavaScript to program their entire stack!\nTo summarize, we have a language that was designed for quick uses, and then grew to a full-fledged tool to write applications with millions of lines. Every language has its own _quirks_ \u2014 oddities and surprises, and JavaScript\u2019s humble beginning makes it have _many_ of these. Some examples:\n  * JavaScript\u2019s equality operator (`==`) _coerces_ its operands, leading to unexpected behavior:\n```\n\njs\n\nif (\"\" == 0) {\n\n\n\n\n  // It is! But why??\n\n\n\n}\n\n\n\nif (1 < x < 3) {\n\n\n\n\n  // True for *any* value of x!\n\n\n\n}\n\n```\n\n  * JavaScript also allows accessing properties which aren\u2019t present:\n```\n\njs\n\nconst obj = { width: 10, height: 15 };\n\n\n\n// Why is this NaN? Spelling is hard!\n\n\n\nconst area = obj.width * obj.heigth;\n\n\n```\n\n\n\nMost programming languages would throw an error when these sorts of errors occur, some would do so during compilation \u2014 before any code is running. When writing small programs, such quirks are annoying but manageable; when writing applications with hundreds or thousands of lines of code, these constant surprises are a serious problem.\n##  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html#typescript-a-static-type-checker)TypeScript: A Static Type Checker\nWe said earlier that some languages wouldn\u2019t allow those buggy programs to run at all. Detecting errors in code without running it is referred to as _static checking_. Determining what\u2019s an error and what\u2019s not based on the kinds of values being operated on is known as static _type_ checking.\nTypeScript checks a program for errors before execution, and does so based on the _kinds of values_ , making it a _static type checker_. For example, the last example above has an error because of the _type_ of `obj`. Here\u2019s the error TypeScript found:\n```\n\nts\n\n\n\nconst obj = { width: 10, height: 15 };\n\n\n\n\nconst area = obj.width * obj.heigth;\n\n\nProperty 'heigth' does not exist on type '{ width: number; height: number; }'. Did you mean 'height'?2551Property 'heigth' does not exist on type '{ width: number; height: number; }'. Did you mean 'height'?Try[](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/#code/PTAEAEFMCdoe2gZwFygEwFYMEYBQBjOAO0QBdQ4AjAK1AF5QBvUAdwEsATUgC1WwAYANKG6Q2Ac26k+GUAF8A3AWJlQAQ2iQ19CjQB07Lt1AAqXdT2iJPBUA)\n\n\n```\n\n###  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html#a-typed-superset-of-javascript)A Typed Superset of JavaScript\nHow does TypeScript relate to JavaScript, though?\n####  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html#syntax)Syntax\nTypeScript is a language that is a _superset_ of JavaScript: JS syntax is therefore legal TS. Syntax refers to the way we write text to form a program. For example, this code has a _syntax_ error because it\u2019s missing a `)`:\n```\n\nts\n\n\n\nlet a = (4\n\n\n')' expected.1005')' expected.Try[](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/#code/PTAEAEFMCdoe2gZwFygIwAYMFYBQAbSAF1AENQBeUACgBYg)\n\n\n```\n\nTypeScript doesn\u2019t consider any JavaScript code to be an error because of its syntax. This means you can take any working JavaScript code and put it in a TypeScript file without worrying about exactly how it is written.\n####  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html#types)Types\nHowever, TypeScript is a _typed_ superset, meaning that it adds rules about how different kinds of values can be used. The earlier error about `obj.heigth` was not a _syntax_ error: it is an error of using some kind of value (a _type_) in an incorrect way.\nAs another example, this is JavaScript code that you can run in your browser, and it _will_ log a value:\n```\n\njs\n\nconsole.log(4 / []);\n\n\n```\n\nThis syntactically-legal program logs `Infinity`. TypeScript, though, considers division of number by an array to be a nonsensical operation, and will issue an error:\n```\n\nts\n\n\n\nconsole.log(4 / []);\n\n\nThe right-hand side of an arithmetic operation must be of type 'any', 'number', 'bigint' or an enum type.2363The right-hand side of an arithmetic operation must be of type 'any', 'number', 'bigint' or an enum type.Try[](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play/#code/PTAEAEFMCdoe2gZwFygEwGYBsGBQBjOAO0TgBtIA6MuAcwAoAWUMAbQF0BKAbiA)\n\n\n```\n\nIt\u2019s possible you really _did_ intend to divide a number by an array, perhaps just to see what happens, but most of the time, though, this is a programming mistake. TypeScript\u2019s type checker is designed to allow correct programs through while still catching as many common errors as possible. (Later, we\u2019ll learn about settings you can use to configure how strictly TypeScript checks your code.)\nIf you move some code from a JavaScript file to a TypeScript file, you might see _type errors_ depending on how the code is written. These may be legitimate problems with the code, or TypeScript being overly conservative. Throughout this guide we\u2019ll demonstrate how to add various TypeScript syntax to eliminate such errors.\n####  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html#runtime-behavior)Runtime Behavior\nTypeScript is also a programming language that preserves the _runtime behavior_ of JavaScript. For example, dividing by zero in JavaScript produces `Infinity` instead of throwing a runtime exception. As a principle, TypeScript **never** changes the runtime behavior of JavaScript code.\nThis means that if you move code from JavaScript to TypeScript, it is **guaranteed** to run the same way, even if TypeScript thinks that the code has type errors.\nKeeping the same runtime behavior as JavaScript is a foundational promise of TypeScript because it means you can easily transition between the two languages without worrying about subtle differences that might make your program stop working.\n####  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html#erased-types)Erased Types\nRoughly speaking, once TypeScript\u2019s compiler is done with checking your code, it _erases_ the types to produce the resulting \u201ccompiled\u201d code. This means that once your code is compiled, the resulting plain JS code has no type information.\nThis also means that TypeScript never changes the _behavior_ of your program based on the types it inferred. The bottom line is that while you might see type errors during compilation, the type system itself has no bearing on how your program works when it runs.\nFinally, TypeScript doesn\u2019t provide any additional runtime libraries. Your programs will use the same standard library (or external libraries) as JavaScript programs, so there\u2019s no additional TypeScript-specific framework to learn.\n##  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html#learning-javascript-and-typescript)Learning JavaScript and TypeScript\nWe frequently see the question \u201cShould I learn JavaScript or TypeScript?\u201c.\nThe answer is that you can\u2019t learn TypeScript without learning JavaScript! TypeScript shares syntax and runtime behavior with JavaScript, so anything you learn about JavaScript is helping you learn TypeScript at the same time.\nThere are many, many resources available for programmers to learn JavaScript; you should _not_ ignore these resources if you\u2019re writing TypeScript. For example, there are about 20 times more StackOverflow questions tagged `javascript` than `typescript`, but _all_ of the `javascript` questions also apply to TypeScript.\nIf you find yourself searching for something like \u201chow to sort a list in TypeScript\u201d, remember: **TypeScript is JavaScript\u2019s runtime with a compile-time type checker**. The way you sort a list in TypeScript is the same way you do so in JavaScript. If you find a resource that uses TypeScript directly, that\u2019s great too, but don\u2019t limit yourself to thinking you need TypeScript-specific answers for everyday questions about how to accomplish runtime tasks.\n##  [](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html#next-steps)Next Steps\nThis was a brief overview of the syntax and tools used in everyday TypeScript. From here, you can:\n  * Learn some of the JavaScript fundamentals, we recommend either:\n    * [Microsoft\u2019s JavaScript Resources](https://developer.microsoft.com/javascript/) or\n    * [JavaScript guide at the Mozilla Web Docs](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide)\n  * Continue to [TypeScript for JavaScript Programmers](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-in-5-minutes.html)\n  * Read the full Handbook [from start to finish](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html)\n  * Explore the [Playground examples](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play#show-examples)\n\n\n##### On this page\n  * [What is JavaScript? A Brief History](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html#what-is-javascript-a-brief-history)\n  * [TypeScript: A Static Type Checker](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html#typescript-a-static-type-checker)\n    * [A Typed Superset of JavaScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html#a-typed-superset-of-javascript)\n  * [Learning JavaScript and TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html#learning-javascript-and-typescript)\n  * [Next Steps](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/typescript-from-scratch.html#next-steps)\n\n\n##### Is this page helpful?\nYesNo\nThe TypeScript docs are an open source project. Help us improve these pages [by sending a Pull Request](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript-Website/blob/v2/packages/documentation/copy/en/get-started/TS%20for%20the%20New%20Programmer.md) and services. We use performance cookies to collect anonymous statistics, so we can understand how customers use our site and make improvements. Essential cookies cannot be deactivated, but you can choose \u201cCustomize\u201d or \u201cDecline\u201d to decline performance cookies.   \n  \nIf you agree, AWS and approved third parties will also use cookies to provide useful site features, remember your preferences, and display relevant content, including relevant advertising. To accept or decline all non-essential cookies, choose \u201cAccept\u201d or \u201cDecline.\u201d To make more detailed choices, choose \u201cCustomize.\u201d\nAcceptDeclineCustomize\n## Customize cookie preferences\nWe use cookies and similar tools (collectively, \"cookies\") for the following purposes.\n### Essential\nEssential cookies are necessary to provide our site and services and cannot be deactivated. They are usually set in response to your actions on the site, such as setting your privacy preferences, signing in, or filling in forms. \n### Performance\nPerformance cookies provide anonymous statistics about how customers navigate our site so we can improve site experience and performance. Approved third parties may perform analytics on our behalf, but they cannot use the data for their own purposes.\nAllowed\n### Functional\nFunctional cookies help us provide useful site features, remember your preferences, and display relevant content. Approved third parties may set these cookies to provide certain site features. If you do not allow these cookies, then some or all of these services may not function properly.\nAllowed\n### Advertising\nAdvertising cookies may be set through our site by us or our advertising partners and help us deliver relevant marketing content. If you do not allow these cookies, you will experience less relevant advertising.\nAllowed\nBlocking some types of cookies may impact your experience of our sites. You may review and change your choices at any time by selecting Cookie preferences in the footer of this site. We and selected third-parties use cookies or similar technologies as specified in the [AWS Cookie Notice](https://aws.amazon.com/legal/cookies/).\nCancelSave preferences\n## Unable to save cookie preferences\nWe will only store essential cookies at this time, because we were unable to save your cookie preferences.  \n  \nIf you want to change your cookie preferences, try again later using the link in the AWS console footer, or contact support if the problem persists.\nDismiss\n[Skip to main content](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#skip-link)\n[English](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html \"Language Selector.
