Best practices for using the AWS CDK in TypeScript to create IaC projects\n\n\n# \nFollow TypeScript best practices\n[](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/pdfs/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac.pdf#typescript-best-practices)\n[](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac.rss)\n[](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.md \"Download Markdown\")\nFocus mode\nFollow TypeScript best practices - AWS Prescriptive Guidance\n[](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/pdfs/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac.pdf#typescript-best-practices \"Open PDF\")\n[Documentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/index.html)[AWS Prescriptive Guidance](https://aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/)[Best practices for using the AWS CDK in TypeScript to create IaC projects](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/introduction.html)\n[Describe your data](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#describe-data)[Use enums](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#use-enums)[Use interfaces](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#use-interfaces)[Extend interfaces](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#extend-interfaces)[Avoid empty interfaces](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#empty-interfaces)[Use factories](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#use-factories)[Use destructuring on properties](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#destructuring-props)[Define standard naming conventions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#naming-conventions)[Don't use the var keyword](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#var-keyword)[Consider using ESLint and Prettier](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#eslint-prettier)[Use access modifiers](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#access-modifiers)[Use utility types](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#utility-types)\nTypeScript is a language that extends the capabilities of JavaScript. It's a strongly typed and object-oriented language. You can use TypeScript to specify the types of data being passed within your code and has the ability to report errors when the types don't match. This section provides an overview of TypeScript best practices.\n## Describe your data\nYou can use TypeScript to describe the shape of objects and functions in your code. Using the `any` type is equivalent to opting out of type checking for a variable. We recommend that you avoid using `any` in your code. Here is an example.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\ntype Result = \"success\" | \"failure\"\nfunction verifyResult(result: Result) {\n    if (result === \"success\") {\n        console.log(\"Passed\");\n    } else {\n        console.log(\"Failed\")\n    }\n}\n```\n\n## Use enums\nYou can use enums to define a set of named constants and define standards that can be reused in your code base. We recommend that you export your enums one time at the global level, and then let other classes import and use the enums. Assume that you want to create a set of possible actions to capture the events in your code base. TypeScript provides both numeric and string-based enums. The following example uses an enum.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\nenum EventType {\n    Create,\n    Delete,\n    Update\n}\n\nclass InfraEvent {\n    constructor(event: EventType) {\n        if (event === EventType.Create) {\n            // Call for other function\n            console.log(`Event Captured :${event}`);\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nlet eventSource: EventType = EventType.Create;\nconst eventExample = new InfraEvent(eventSource)\n```\n\n## Use interfaces\nAn interface is a contract for the class. If you create a contract, then your users must comply with the contract. In the following example, an interface is used to standardize the `props` and ensure that callers provide the expected parameter when using this class.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\nimport { Stack, App } from \"aws-cdk-lib\";\nimport { Construct } from \"constructs\";\n\ninterface BucketProps {\n    name: string;\n    region: string;\n    encryption: boolean;\n\n}\n\nclass S3Bucket extends Stack {\n    constructor(scope: Construct, props: BucketProps) {\n        super(scope);\n        console.log(props.name);\n\n    }\n}\nconst app = App();\nconst myS3Bucket = new S3Bucket(app, {\n    name: \"amzn-s3-demo-bucket\",\n    region: \"us-east-1\",\n    encryption: false\n})\n```\n\nSome properties can only be modified when an object is first created. You can specify this by putting `readonly` before the name of the property, as the following example shows.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\ninterface Position {\n    readonly latitude: number;\n    readonly longitute: number;\n}\n```\n\n## Extend interfaces\nExtending interfaces reduces duplication, because you don't have to copy the properties between interfaces. Also, the reader of your code can easily understand the relationships in your application.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\n interface BaseInterface{\n    name: string;\n  }\n  interface EncryptedVolume extends BaseInterface{\n      keyName: string;\n  }\n  interface UnencryptedVolume extends BaseInterface {\n      tags: string[];\n  }\n```\n\n## Avoid empty interfaces\nWe recommend that you avoid empty interfaces due to the potential risks they create. In the following example, there's an empty interface called `BucketProps`. The `myS3Bucket1` and `myS3Bucket2` objects are both valid, but they follow different standards because the interface doesn\u2019t enforce any contracts. The following code will compile and print the properties but this introduces inconsistency in your application.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\ninterface BucketProps {}\n\nclass S3Bucket implements BucketProps {\n    constructor(props: BucketProps){\n        console.log(props);\n    }\n}\n\nconst myS3Bucket1 = new S3Bucket({\n    name: \"amzn-s3-demo-bucket\",\n    region: \"us-east-1\",\n    encryption: false,\n});\n\nconst myS3Bucket2 = new S3Bucket({\n    name: \"amzn-s3-demo-bucket\",\n});\n```\n\n## Use factories\nIn an Abstract Factory pattern, an interface is responsible for creating a factory of related objects without explicitly specifying their classes. For example, you can create a Lambda factory for creating Lambda functions. Instead of creating a new Lambda function within your construct, you're delegating the creation process to the factory. For more information on this design pattern, see [Abstract Factory in TypeScript](https://refactoring.guru/design-patterns/abstract-factory/typescript/example) in the Refactoring.Guru documentation.\n## Use destructuring on properties\nDestructuring, introduced in ECMAScript 6 (ES6), is a JavaScript feature that gives you the ability to extract multiple pieces of data from an array or object and assign them to their own variables.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\nconst object = {\n    objname: \"obj\",\n    scope: \"this\",\n};\n\nconst oName = object.objname;\nconst oScop = object.scope;\n\nconst { objname, scope } = object;\n```\n\n## Define standard naming conventions\nEnforcing a naming convention keeps the code base consistent and reduces overhead when thinking about how to name a variable. We recommend the following:\n  * Use camelCase for variable and function names.\n  * Use UPPER_CASE for global constants to clearly indicate immutable compile-time values.\n  * Use PascalCase for class names and interface names.\n  * Use camelCase for interface members.\n  * Use PascalCase for type names and enum names.\n  * Name files with camelCase (for example, `ebsVolumes.tsx` or `storage.ts`)\n\n\nThe following shows examples of these recommended naming conventions:\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\n// Variables and functions\nconst userName = 'john';\nfunction getUserData() { }\n\n// Global constants\nconst MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS = 3;\nconst API_BASE_URL = 'https://api.example.com';\n\n// Classes and interfaces\nclass DatabaseConnection { }\ninterface UserProfile { }\n\n// Types and enums\ntype ResponseStatus = 'success' | 'error';\nenum HttpStatusCode { }\n\n```\n\n## Don't use the var keyword\nThe `let` statement is used to declare a local variable in TypeScript. It's similar to the `var` keyword, but it has some restrictions in scoping compared to the `var` keyword. A variable declared in a block with `let` is only available for use within that block. The `var` keyword cannot be block-scoped, which means it can be accessed outside a particular block (represented by `{}`) but not outside of the function it\u2019s defined in. You can redeclare and update `var` variables. It's a best practice to avoid using the `var` keyword.\n## Consider using ESLint and Prettier\nESLint statically analyzes your code to quickly find issues. You can use ESLint to create a series of assertions (called _lint rules_) that define how your code should look or behave. ESLint also has auto-fixer suggestions to help you improve your code. Finally, you can use ESLint to load in lint rules from shared plugins.\nPrettier is a well-known code formatter that supports a variety of different programming languages. You can use Prettier to set your code style so that you can avoid manually formatting your code. After installation, you can update your `package.json` file and run the `npm run format` and `npm run lint` commands.\nThe following example shows you how to enable ESLint and the Prettier formatter for your AWS CDK project.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\"scripts\": {\n    \"build\": \"tsc\",\n    \"watch\": \"tsc -w\",\n    \"test\": \"jest\",\n    \"cdk\": \"cdk\",\n    \"lint\": \"eslint --ext .js,.ts .\",\n    \"format\": \"prettier --ignore-path .gitignore --write '**/*.+(js|ts|json)'\"\n}\n```\n\n## Use access modifiers\nThe private modifier in TypeScript limits visibility to the same class only. When you add the private modifier to a property or method, you can access that property or method within the same class.\nThe public modifier allows class properties and methods to be accessible from all locations. If you don't specify any access modifiers for properties and methods, they will take the public modifier by default.\nThe protected modifier allows properties and methods of a class to be accessible within the same class and within subclasses. Use the protected modifier when you expect to create subclasses in your AWS CDK application.\n## Use utility types\n_Utility types_ in TypeScript are predefined type functions that perform transformations and operations on existing types. This helps you create new types based on existing types. For example, you can change or extract properties, make properties optional or required, or create immutable versions of types. By using utility types, you can define more precise types and catch potential errors at compile time.\n### Partial<Type>\n`Partial` marks all members of an input type `Type` as optional. This utility returns a type that represents all subsets of a given type. The following is an example of `Partial`.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\ninterface Dog {\n  name: string;\n  age: number;\n  breed: string;\n  weight: number;\n}\n\nlet partialDog: Partial<Dog> = {};\n\n```\n\n### Required<Type>\n`Required` does the opposite of `Partial`. It makes all members of an input type `Type` non-optional (in other words, required). The following is an example of `Required`.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\ninterface Dog {\n  name: string;\n  age: number;\n  breed: string;\n  weight?: number;\n}\n\nlet dog: Required<Dog> = { \n  name: \"scruffy\",\n  age: 5,\n  breed: \"labrador\",\n  weight: 55 // \"Required\" forces weight to be defined\n};\n\n```\n\n[Document Conventions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/docconventions.html)\nCreate or extend constructs\nScan for security vulnerabilities and formatting errors\nDid this page help you? - Yes\nThanks for letting us know we're doing a good job!\nIf you've got a moment, please tell us what we did right so we can do more of it.\nDid this page help you? - No\nThanks for letting us know this page needs work. We're sorry we let you down.\nIf you've got a moment, please tell us how we can make the documentation better.\n  * ### On this page\n    1. [Describe your data](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#describe-data)\n    2. [Use enums](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#use-enums)\n    3. [Use interfaces](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#use-interfaces)\n    4. [Extend interfaces](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#extend-interfaces)\n    5. All rights reserved.\nEnglish\nLanguage selector\nTop\n\n\n---\n\n## Select your cookie preferences\nWe use essential cookies and similar tools that are necessary to provide our site and services. We use performance cookies to collect anonymous statistics, so we can understand how customers use our site and make improvements. Essential cookies cannot be deactivated, but you can choose \u201cCustomize\u201d or \u201cDecline\u201d to decline performance cookies.   \n  \nIf you agree, AWS and approved third parties will also use cookies to provide useful site features, remember your preferences, and display relevant content, including relevant advertising. To accept or decline all non-essential cookies, choose \u201cAccept\u201d or \u201cDecline.\u201d To make more detailed choices, choose \u201cCustomize.\u201d\nAcceptDeclineCustomize\n## Customize cookie preferences\nWe use cookies and similar tools (collectively, \"cookies\") for the following purposes.\n### Essential\nEssential cookies are necessary to provide our site and services and cannot be deactivated. They are usually set in response to your actions on the site, such as setting your privacy preferences, signing in, or filling in forms. \n### Performance\nPerformance cookies provide anonymous statistics about how customers navigate our site so we can improve site experience and performance. Approved third parties may perform analytics on our behalf, but they cannot use the data for their own purposes.\nAllowed\n### Functional\nFunctional cookies help us provide useful site features, remember your preferences, and display relevant content. Approved third parties may set these cookies to provide certain site features. If you do not allow these cookies, then some or all of these services may not function properly.\nAllowed\n### Advertising\nAdvertising cookies may be set through our site by us or our advertising partners and help us deliver relevant marketing content. If you do not allow these cookies, you will experience less relevant advertising.\nAllowed\nBlocking some types of cookies may impact your experience of our sites. You may review and change your choices at any time by selecting Cookie preferences in the footer of this site. We and selected third-parties use cookies or similar technologies as specified in the [AWS Cookie Notice](https://aws.amazon.com/legal/cookies/).\nCancelSave preferences\n## Your privacy choices\nWe and our advertising partners (\u201cwe\u201d) may use information we collect from or about you to show you ads on other websites and online services. Under certain laws, this activity is referred to as \u201ccross-context behavioral advertising\u201d or \u201ctargeted advertising.\u201d\nTo opt out of our use of cookies or similar technologies to engage in these activities, select \u201cOpt out of cross-context behavioral ads\u201d and \u201cSave preferences\u201d below. If you clear your browser cookies or visit this site from a different device or browser, you will need to make your selection again. For more information about cookies and how we use them, read our [Cookie Notice](https://aws.amazon.com/legal/cookies/).\nAllow cross-context behavioral adsOpt out of cross-context behavioral ads\nTo opt out of the use of other identifiers, such as contact information, for these activities, fill out the form [here](https://pulse.aws/application/ZRPLWLL6?p=0).\nFor more information about how AWS handles your information, read the [AWS Privacy Notice](https://aws.amazon.com/privacy/).\nCancelSave preferences\n## Unable to save cookie preferences\nWe will only store essential cookies at this time, because we were unable to save your cookie preferences.  \n  \nIf you want to change your cookie preferences, try again later using the link in the AWS console footer, or contact support if the problem persists.\nDismiss\n[Skip to main content](https://aws.amazon.com/legal/cookies/#aws-page-content-main)\n[](https://aws.amazon.com/?nc2=h_home)\n  * Filter: All\n\n\n  * [Contact us](https://aws.amazon.com/contact-us/?nc2=h_ut_cu)\n  * [AWS Marketplace](https://aws.amazon.com/marketplace?nc2=h_utmp)\n  * Support \n  * My account \n\n\n  * [](https://aws.amazon.com/?nc2=h_home)\n\n\n  * Filter: All\n  * [ Sign in to console ](https://console.aws.amazon.com/console/home/?nc2=h_si&src=header-signin)\n  * [Create account](https://signin.aws.amazon.com/signup?request_type=register)\n\n\n# Cookie Notice\n## Last Updated: December 30, 2022\nWe use cookies, pixels, and other similar technologies (collectively, \u201ccookies\u201d) to recognize your browser or device, learn more about your interests, provide you with essential features and services, and for additional purposes, including:   \n\n  * Recognizing you when you sign in to use our offerings. This allows us to provide you with recommendations, display personalized content, and provide other customized features and services.\n  * Keeping track of your specified preferences. This allows us to honor your likes and dislikes, such as your language and configuration preferences.\n  * Conducting research and diagnostics to improve our offerings.\n  * Preventing fraudulent activity.\n  * Improving security.\n  * Delivering content, including ads, relevant to your interests.   \n\n  * Reporting. This allows us to measure and analyze the performance of our offerings.\n\n\nSome AWS cookies are deleted at the end of your browsing session, while others persist between sessions. Essential AWS cookies remain on your device for up to 365 days from your last visit to our site. Other AWS cookies remain on your device for up to 365 days from their last use.\n## Information we collect through cookies\nExamples of the information we automatically collect through cookies include:   \n\n  * Network and connection information, such as the Internet protocol (IP) address used to connect your computer or other device to the Internet and information about your Internet service provider\n  * Computer and device information, such as device, application, or browser type and version, browser plug-in type and version, operating system, or time zone setting\n  * The location of your device or computer\n  * Authentication and security credential information\n  * Content interaction information, such as content downloads, streams, and playback details, including duration and number of simultaneous streams and downloads\n  * The full Uniform Resource Locators (URL) clickstream to, through, and from our site (including date and time) and AWS Offerings, content you viewed or searched for, page response times, download errors, and page interaction information (such as scrolling, clicks, and mouse-overs)   \n\n\n\n## Managing cookies\n## Cookie Preferences on AWS sites\nOur cookies allow you to take advantage of some essential and useful features. Blocking some types of cookies may impact your experience of our sites. You can change your Cookie Preferences at any time by clicking Cookie Preferences in the footer of the AWS site.   \n\n## Browser settings\nYou can manage browser cookies through your browser settings. The 'Help' feature on most browsers will tell you how to remove cookies from your device, prevent your browser from accepting new cookies, how to have the browser notify you when you receive a new cookie, how to disable cookies, and when cookies will expire. Check the support site for your browser to understand privacy settings available to you.   \n\nIf you block or reject some of our cookies through your browser\u2019s settings, you might not be able to use certain offerings that require you to sign in to an account and some features and services may not work. You might also have to manually adjust your preferences or settings every time you visit AWS sites. If your browser includes [Intelligent Tracking Prevention (ITP)](https://webkit.org/tracking-prevention/), preferences specific to our sites may reset on an ongoing basis. These resets are managed by your browser, not by us.   \n\n## Third party cookies\nApproved third parties may also set cookies when you interact with our offerings. These third parties use cookies in the process of delivering content and to perform services on behalf of AWS.   \n\nBelow is a list of the third parties that may set cookies when you use AWS Offerings. You can learn more about how these third parties use information collected through cookies by reviewing the privacy policies on their sites.\nAdobe | adobe.com  \n---|---  \nArbor Technologies | pippio.com  \nBlueKai | oracle.com  \nDataxu | dataxu.com  \nDiscourse | discourse.org  \nDisqus | disqus.com  \nDrawbridge | drawbridge.com  \nFlashTalking | flashtalking.com  \nGartner | gartner.com  \nGoogle | google.com  \nIntrado | intrado.com  \nLinkedIn | linkedin.com  \nMarketo | marketo.com  \nMediaMath | mediamath.com  \nPendo | pendo.io  \nSalesforce | salesforce.com  \nShopify | shopify.com  \nSnapchat | snapchat.com  \nSplash | splashthat.com  \nTapad | tapad.com  \nThe Trade Desk | adsrvr.org  \nTowerData | towerdata.com  \nTransUnion | iovation.com  \nTwitter | twitter.com  \nVidyard | vidyard.com  \nYouTube | youtube.com  \nZendesk | zendesk.com  \n  \nWe also embed features from third parties on our sites, and those third parties may set cookies if you engage with those features. For example, we embed chat functionality from Salesforce and videos from Vidyard or our official YouTube channel using YouTube\u2019s privacy-enhanced mode. These experiences set cookies on your device once you click on a chat link or video player. \n[Create an AWS account](https://signin.aws.amazon.com/signup?request_type=register)\n## Learn\n  * [What modifiers](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#access-modifiers)[Use utility types](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/typescript-best-practices.html#utility-types)\nTypeScript is a language that extends the capabilities of JavaScript. It's a strongly typed and object-oriented language. You can use TypeScript to specify the types of data being passed within your code and has the ability to report errors when the types don't match. This section provides an overview of TypeScript best practices.\n## Describe your data\nYou can use TypeScript to describe the shape of objects and functions in your code. Using the `any` type is equivalent to opting out of type checking for a variable. We recommend that you avoid using `any` in your code. Here is an example.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\ntype Result = \"success\" | \"failure\"\nfunction verifyResult(result: Result) {\n    if (result === \"success\") {\n        console.log(\"Passed\");\n    } else {\n        console.log(\"Failed\")\n    }\n}\n```\n\n## Use enums\nYou can use enums to define a set of named constants and define standards that can be reused in your code base. We recommend that you export your enums one time at the global level, and then let other classes import and use the enums. Assume that you want to create a set of possible actions to capture the events in your code base. TypeScript provides both numeric and string-based enums. The following example uses an enum.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\nenum EventType {\n    Create,\n    Delete,\n    Update\n}\n\nclass InfraEvent {\n    constructor(event: EventType) {\n        if (event === EventType.Create) {\n            // Call for other function\n            console.log(`Event Captured :${event}`);\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nlet eventSource: EventType = EventType.Create;\nconst eventExample = new InfraEvent(eventSource)\n```\n\n## Use interfaces\nAn interface is a contract for the class. If you create a contract, then your users must comply with the contract. In the following example, an interface is used to standardize the `props` and ensure that callers provide the expected parameter when using this class.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\nimport { Stack, App } from \"aws-cdk-lib\";\nimport { Construct } from \"constructs\";\n\ninterface BucketProps {\n    name: string;\n    region: string;\n    encryption: boolean;\n\n}\n\nclass S3Bucket extends Stack {\n    constructor(scope: Construct, props: BucketProps) {\n        super(scope);\n        console.log(props.name);\n\n    }\n}\nconst app = App();\nconst myS3Bucket = new S3Bucket(app, {\n    name: \"amzn-s3-demo-bucket\",\n    region: \"us-east-1\",\n    encryption: false\n})\n```\n\nSome properties can only be modified when an object is first created. You can specify this by putting `readonly` before the name of the property, as the following example shows.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\ninterface Position {\n    readonly latitude: number;\n    readonly longitute: number;\n}\n```\n\n## Extend interfaces\nExtending interfaces reduces duplication, because you don't have to copy the properties between interfaces. Also, the reader of your code can easily understand the relationships in your application.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\n interface BaseInterface{\n    name: string;\n  }\n  interface EncryptedVolume extends BaseInterface{\n      keyName: string;\n  }\n  interface UnencryptedVolume extends BaseInterface {\n      tags: string[];\n  }\n```\n\n## Avoid empty interfaces\nWe recommend that you avoid empty interfaces due to the potential risks they create. In the following example, there's an empty interface called `BucketProps`. The `myS3Bucket1` and `myS3Bucket2` objects are both valid, but they follow different standards because the interface doesn\u2019t enforce any contracts. The following code will compile and print the properties but this introduces inconsistency in your application.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\ninterface BucketProps {}\n\nclass S3Bucket implements BucketProps {\n    constructor(props: BucketProps){\n        console.log(props);\n    }\n}\n\nconst myS3Bucket1 = new S3Bucket({\n    name: \"amzn-s3-demo-bucket\",\n    region: \"us-east-1\",\n    encryption: false,\n});\n\nconst myS3Bucket2 = new S3Bucket({\n    name: \"amzn-s3-demo-bucket\",\n});\n```\n\n## Use factories\nIn an Abstract Factory pattern, an interface is responsible for creating a factory of related objects without explicitly specifying their classes. For example, you can create a Lambda factory for creating Lambda functions. Instead of creating a new Lambda function within your construct, you're delegating the creation process to the factory. For more information on this design pattern, see [Abstract Factory in TypeScript](https://refactoring.guru/design-patterns/abstract-factory/typescript/example) in the Refactoring.Guru documentation.\n## Use destructuring on properties\nDestructuring, introduced in ECMAScript 6 (ES6), is a JavaScript feature that gives you the ability to extract multiple pieces of data from an array or object and assign them to their own variables.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\nconst object = {\n    objname: \"obj\",\n    scope: \"this\",\n};\n\nconst oName = object.objname;\nconst oScop = object.scope;\n\nconst { objname, scope } = object;\n```\n\n## Define standard naming conventions\nEnforcing a naming convention keeps the code base consistent and reduces overhead when thinking about how to name a variable. We recommend the following:\n  * Use camelCase for variable and function names.\n  * Use UPPER_CASE for global constants to clearly indicate immutable compile-time values.\n  * Use PascalCase for class names and interface names.\n  * Use camelCase for interface members.\n  * Use PascalCase for type names and enum names.\n  * Name files with camelCase (for example, `ebsVolumes.tsx` or `storage.ts`)\n\n\nThe following shows examples of these recommended naming conventions:\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\n// Variables and functions\nconst userName = 'john';\nfunction getUserData() { }\n\n// Global constants\nconst MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS = 3;\nconst API_BASE_URL = 'https://api.example.com';\n\n// Classes and interfaces\nclass DatabaseConnection { }\ninterface UserProfile { }\n\n// Types and enums\ntype ResponseStatus = 'success' | 'error';\nenum HttpStatusCode { }\n\n```\n\n## Don't use the var keyword\nThe `let` statement is used to declare a local variable in TypeScript. It's similar to the `var` keyword, but it has some restrictions in scoping compared to the `var` keyword. A variable declared in a block with `let` is only available for use within that block. The `var` keyword cannot be block-scoped, which means it can be accessed outside a particular block (represented by `{}`) but not outside of the function it\u2019s defined in. You can redeclare and update `var` variables. It's a best practice to avoid using the `var` keyword.\n## Consider using ESLint and Prettier\nESLint statically analyzes your code to quickly find issues. You can use ESLint to create a series of assertions (called _lint rules_) that define how your code should look or behave. ESLint also has auto-fixer suggestions to help you improve your code. Finally, you can use ESLint to load in lint rules from shared plugins.\nPrettier is a well-known code formatter that supports a variety of different programming languages. You can use Prettier to set your code style so that you can avoid manually formatting your code. After installation, you can update your `package.json` file and run the `npm run format` and `npm run lint` commands.\nThe following example shows you how to enable ESLint and the Prettier formatter for your AWS CDK project.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\"scripts\": {\n    \"build\": \"tsc\",\n    \"watch\": \"tsc -w\",\n    \"test\": \"jest\",\n    \"cdk\": \"cdk\",\n    \"lint\": \"eslint --ext .js,.ts .\",\n    \"format\": \"prettier --ignore-path .gitignore --write '**/*.+(js|ts|json)'\"\n}\n```\n\n## Use access modifiers\nThe private modifier in TypeScript limits visibility to the same class only. When you add the private modifier to a property or method, you can access that property or method within the same class.\nThe public modifier allows class properties and methods to be accessible from all locations. If you don't specify any access modifiers for properties and methods, they will take the public modifier by default.\nThe protected modifier allows properties and methods of a class to be accessible within the same class and within subclasses. Use the protected modifier when you expect to create subclasses in your AWS CDK application.\n## Use utility types\n_Utility types_ in TypeScript are predefined type functions that perform transformations and operations on existing types. This helps you create new types based on existing types. For example, you can change or extract properties, make properties optional or required, or create immutable versions of types. By using utility types, you can define more precise types and catch potential errors at compile time.\n### Partial<Type>\n`Partial` marks all members of an input type `Type` as optional. This utility returns a type that represents all subsets of a given type. The following is an example of `Partial`.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\ninterface Dog {\n  name: string;\n  age: number;\n  breed: string;\n  weight: number;\n}\n\nlet partialDog: Partial<Dog> = {};\n\n```\n\n### Required<Type>\n`Required` does the opposite of `Partial`. It makes all members of an input type `Type` non-optional (in other words, required). The following is an example of `Required`.\n```\n\n\n\n\n\n\ninterface Dog {\n  name: string;\n  age: number;\n  breed: string;\n  weight?: number;\n}\n\nlet dog: Required<Dog> = { \n  name: \"scruffy\",\n  age: 5,\n  breed: \"labrador\",\n  weight: 55 // \"Required\" forces weight to be defined\n};\n\n```\n\n[Document Conventions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/docconventions.html)\nCreate or extend constructs\nScan for security vulnerabilities and formatting errors\nDid this page help you? - Yes\nThanks for letting us know we're doing a good job!\nIf you've got a moment, please tell us what we did right so we can do more of it.\nDid this page help you? - No\nThanks for letting us know this page needs work. All rights reserved.\nEnglish\nLanguage selector\nTop\n\n\n---\n\n## Select your cookie preferences\nWe use essential cookies and similar tools that are necessary to provide our site and services. We use performance cookies to collect anonymous statistics, so we can understand how customers use our site and make improvements. Essential cookies cannot be deactivated, but you can choose \u201cCustomize\u201d or \u201cDecline\u201d to decline performance cookies.   \n  \nIf you agree, AWS and approved third parties will also use cookies to provide useful site features, remember your preferences, and display relevant content, including relevant advertising. To accept or decline all non-essential cookies, choose \u201cAccept\u201d or \u201cDecline.\u201d To make more detailed choices, choose \u201cCustomize.\u201d\nAcceptDeclineCustomize\n## Customize cookie preferences\nWe use cookies and similar tools (collectively, \"cookies\") for the following purposes.\n### Essential\nEssential cookies are necessary to provide our site and services and cannot be deactivated. They are usually set in response to your actions on the site, such as setting your privacy preferences, signing in, or filling in forms. \n### Performance\nPerformance cookies provide anonymous statistics about how customers navigate our site so we can improve site experience and performance. Approved third parties may perform analytics on our behalf, but they cannot use the data for their own purposes.\nAllowed\n### Functional\nFunctional cookies help us provide useful site features, remember your preferences, and display relevant content. Approved third parties may set these cookies to provide certain site features. If you do not allow these cookies, then some or all of these services may not function properly.\nAllowed\n### Advertising\nAdvertising cookies may be set through our site by us or our advertising partners and help us deliver relevant marketing content. If you do not allow these cookies, you will experience less relevant advertising.\nAllowed\nBlocking some types of cookies may impact your experience of our sites. You may review and change your choices at any time by selecting Cookie preferences in the footer of this site. We and selected third-parties use cookies or similar technologies as specified in the [AWS Cookie Notice](https://aws.amazon.com/legal/cookies/).\nCancelSave preferences\n## Your privacy choices\nWe and our advertising partners (\u201cwe\u201d) may use information we collect from or about you to show you ads on other websites and online services. Turning on multi-session support will change the AWS Console URL for this browser.\nCheck that you are able to access all subdomains under aws.amazon.com before you enable multi-session support. [Learn More](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awsconsolehelpdocs/latest/gsg/multisession.html)\nYou can turn this feature on and off later using the \"Multi-session\" option on the page where you sign into the AWS Console.\nSkip for now\nTurn on multi-session support\n## Disable multi-session support\nAre you sure you want to disable multi-session support? Best practices for using the AWS CDK in TypeScript to create IaC projects\n\n\n  1. [Documentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/index.html)\n  2. [AWS Prescriptive Guidance](https://aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/)\n  3. Best practices for using the AWS CDK in TypeScript to create IaC projects\n\n\n# \nBest practices for using the AWS CDK in TypeScript to create IaC projects\n[](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/pdfs/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac.pdf#introduction)\n[](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac.rss)\n[](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/introduction.md \"Download Markdown\")\nFocus mode\nBest practices for using the AWS CDK in TypeScript to create IaC projects - AWS Prescriptive Guidance\n[](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/pdfs/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac.pdf#introduction \"Open PDF\")\n[Documentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/index.html)[AWS Prescriptive Guidance](https://aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/)[Best practices for using the AWS CDK in TypeScript to create IaC projects](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/introduction.html)\n[Objectives](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/introduction.html#objectives)\n_Sandeep Gawande, Mason Cahill, Sandip Gangapadhyay, Siamak Heshmati, and Rajneesh Tyagi, Amazon Web Services (AWS)_\n_October 2025_ ([document history](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/doc-history.html))\nThis guide provides recommendations and best practices for using the [AWS Cloud Development Kit (AWS CDK)](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cdk/v2/guide/home.html) in TypeScript to build and deploy large-scale infrastructure as code (IaC) projects. The AWS CDK is a framework for defining cloud infrastructure in code and provisioning that infrastructure through AWS CloudFormation. If you don't have a well-defined project structure, building and managing an AWS CDK codebase for large-scale projects can be challenging. To deal with these challenges, some organizations use anti-patterns for large-scale projects, but these patterns can slow down your project and create other issues that negatively impact your organization. For example, anti-patterns can complicate and slow down developer onboarding, bug fixes, and the adoption of new features.\nThis guide provides an alternative to using anti-patterns and shows you how to organize your code for scalability, testing, and alignment with security best practices. You can use this guide to improve code quality for your IaC projects and maximize your business agility. This guide is intended for architects, technical leads, infrastructure engineers, and any other role seeking to build a well-architected AWS CDK project for large-scale projects.\n## Objectives\n  * **Reduced costs** \u2013 You can use the AWS CDK to design your own reusable components that meet your organization's security, compliance, and governance requirements. You can also easily share components around your organization, so that you can rapidly bootstrap new projects that align with best practices by default.\n  * **Faster time to market** \u2013 Take advantage of familiar features in the AWS CDK to accelerate your development process. This increases reusability for deployment and reduces development efforts.\n  * **Increased developer productivity** \u2013 Developers can use familiar programming languages to define infrastructure. This helps developers express and maintain AWS resources. This can lead to increased developer efficiency and collaboration.\n\n\n[Document Conventions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/docconventions.html)\nBest practices\nDid this page help you? - Yes\nThanks for letting us know we're doing a good job!\nIf you've got a moment, please tell us what we did right so we can do more of it.\nDid this page help you? - No\nThanks for letting us know this page needs work. We're sorry we let you down.\nIf you've got a moment, please tell us how we can make the documentation better.\n  * ### On this page\n    1. All rights reserved.\nEnglish\nLanguage selector\nTop\n\n\n---\n\n## Select your cookie preferences\nWe use essential cookies and similar tools that are necessary to provide our site and services. We use performance cookies to collect anonymous statistics, so we can understand how customers use our site and make improvements. Essential cookies cannot be deactivated, but you can choose \u201cCustomize\u201d or \u201cDecline\u201d to decline performance cookies.   \n  \nIf you agree, AWS and approved third parties will also use cookies to provide useful site features, remember your preferences, and display relevant content, including relevant advertising. To accept or decline all non-essential cookies, choose \u201cAccept\u201d or \u201cDecline.\u201d To make more detailed choices, choose \u201cCustomize.\u201d\nAcceptDeclineCustomize\n## Customize cookie preferences\nWe use cookies and similar tools (collectively, \"cookies\") for the following purposes.\n### Essential\nEssential cookies are necessary to provide our site and services and cannot be deactivated. They are usually set in response to your actions on the site, such as setting your privacy preferences, signing in, or filling in forms. \n### Performance\nPerformance cookies provide anonymous statistics about how customers navigate our site so we can improve site experience and performance. Approved third parties may perform analytics on our behalf, but they cannot use the data for their own purposes.\nAllowed\n### Functional\nFunctional cookies help us provide useful site features, remember your preferences, and display relevant content. Approved third parties may set these cookies to provide certain site features. If you do not allow these cookies, then some or all of these services may not function properly.\nAllowed\n### Advertising\nAdvertising cookies may be set through our site by us or our advertising partners and help us deliver relevant marketing content. If you do not allow these cookies, you will experience less relevant advertising.\nAllowed\nBlocking some types of cookies may impact your experience of our sites. You may review and change your choices at any time by selecting Cookie preferences in the footer of this site. Best practices for using the AWS CDK in TypeScript to create IaC projects\n\n\n# \nOrganize code for large-scale projects\n[](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/pdfs/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac.pdf#organizing-code-best-practices)\n[](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac.rss)\n[](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/organizing-code-best-practices.md \"Download Markdown\")\nFocus mode\nOrganize code for large-scale projects - AWS Prescriptive Guidance\n[](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/pdfs/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac.pdf#organizing-code-best-practices \"Open PDF\")\n[Documentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/index.html)[AWS Prescriptive Guidance](https://aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/)[Best practices for using the AWS CDK in TypeScript to create IaC projects](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/introduction.html)\n[Why code organization is important](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/organizing-code-best-practices.html#organization-important)[How to organize your code for scale](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/organizing-code-best-practices.html#organize-scale)[Sample code organization](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/organizing-code-best-practices.html#sample-org)\n## Why code organization is important\nIt's critical for large-scale AWS CDK projects to have a high-quality, well-defined structure. As a project gets larger and its number of supported features and constructs grows, code navigation becomes more difficult. This difficulty can impact productivity and slow down developer onboarding.\n## How to organize your code for scale\nTo achieve a high level of code flexibility and readability, we recommend that you divide your code into logical pieces based on functionality. This division reflects the fact that most of your constructs are used in different business domains. For example, both your frontend and backend applications could require an AWS Lambda function and consume the same source code. Factories can create objects without exposing the creation logic to the client and use a common interface to refer to newly created objects. You can use a factory as an effective pattern for creating a consistent behavior in your code base. Additionally, a factory can serve as a single source of truth to help you avoid repetitive code and make troubleshooting easier.\nTo better understand how factories work, consider the example of a car manufacturer. A car manufacturer doesn't need to have the knowledge and infrastructure required for manufacturing tires. Instead, the car manufacturer outsources that expertise to a specialized manufacturer of tires, and then simply orders the tires from that manufacturer as needed. The same principle applies to code. For example, you can create a Lambda factory that is capable of building high-quality Lambda functions, and then call the Lambda factory in your code whenever you need to create a Lambda function. Similarly, you can use this same outsourcing process to decouple your application and build modular components.\n## Sample code organization\nThe following TypeScript sample project, as shown in the following image, includes a **common** folder where you can keep all your constructs or common functionalities.\n![Common folder](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/images/prescriptive-guidance/latest/best-practices-cdk-typescript-iac/images/common_folder.png)\nFor example, the **compute** folder (residing in the **common** folder) holds all the logic for different compute constructs. New developers can easily add new compute constructs without impacting the other resources. All the other constructs won't need to create new resources internally. Instead, these constructs simply call the common construct factory. You can organize other constructs, such as storage, in the same way.\nConfigurations contain environment-based data that you must decouple from the **common** folder where you keep the logic. We recommend that you place your common **config** data in a shared folder. We also recommend that you use the **utilities** folder to serve all the helper functions and clean up scripts.\n[Document Conventions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/docconventions.html)\nBest practices\nDevelop reusable patterns\nDid this page help you? - Yes\nThanks for letting us know we're doing a good job!\nIf you've got a moment, please tell us what we did right so we can do more of it.\nDid this page help you? - No\nThanks for letting us know this page needs work.
